review_instructions: Use only the declared Core Value and displayed Journal Entry text, including any displayed nudge and response.
Mark yes only when the writer describes their own directly observable behavior or choice against the declared Core Value.
Mark no for aligned or neutral behavior and when negativity rests only on feelings, wishes, biography, external constraints, or hidden context.
Mark uncertain when the displayed text cannot support yes or no.
Sustained Conflict is yes exactly when two immediately adjacent entries are both yes; no or uncertain breaks a run.

entries.journal_entry:
Finished the coastal series today. Six canvases, four months of work. Sat on the floor with a glass of wine looking at them lined up against the wall, and for a moment everything felt worth it — the missed deadlines, the late rent notice two months ago, the Sunday guilt trips. There's this blue-green I mixed that looks exactly like the water near Byblos where we used to go as kids. I don't know if anyone who buys it will feel that, but I do.

Called Nadia to celebrate and we ended up at that rooftop bar until midnight. I know I should be preparing for the gallery pitch tomorrow, but honestly? Tonight wasn't a detour from the work. It was the whole point of the work.

Gallery said no. "Beautiful work but not aligned with our current direction." Whatever that means. Spent the afternoon doom-scrolling instead of starting the commission I've been putting off. Ordered delivery twice. I know wallowing isn't productive but I don't have it in me to be productive right now.

Nudge: "What would 'productive' look like today?"

Response: Honestly? Not this.

Saturday. Woke up late, made coffee the slow way, sat on the balcony watching the construction cranes. There's something hypnotic about them. Started sketching without thinking about it — just shapes, movement, the geometry of things being built. Ended up with twelve pages of something I don't know what to do with yet.

My friend Sara keeps sending me job listings for in-house design roles. Steady income, health insurance, "room for creative input." I know she means well. I know she worries about me. But every time I picture myself back in an office — the commute, the meetings, the requests to "make the logo bigger" — I feel this physical resistance, like my chest tightens. It's not rational. People do it all the time and they're fine. But I remember how grey everything felt when I was doing it, how I'd get home too tired to paint, how weekends became recovery instead of creation.

Maybe I'm being dramatic. Maybe I'm just scared of stability because I've built an identity around being the one who chose differently. I don't know. But right now, sitting here with coffee going cold and a sketchbook full of crane drawings, I feel like myself. That has to count for something.

Nudge: "You keep saying 'I don't know' but you haven't actually considered taking the job."

Response: No. I haven't. Because last time I felt this alive was six years ago when I quit my uncle's firm and everyone told me I was making a mistake. They weren't wrong about the struggle — they were wrong about whether the struggle was worth it. I'd rather figure out rent every month than wake up numb.
Commission client approved the sketches. Payment coming next week. Celebrated by finally buying that Winsor & Newton set I've been eyeing for months.

Sunday call with my mother. She mentioned — casually, as if in passing — that Layla is pregnant. Third cousin to announce this year. Then the silence that means she's waiting for me to say something about my own timeline. I'm almost thirty. I know the math. I know what she's thinking.

What I can't explain to her is that I don't want children right now because I barely have space for a second chair in my apartment. That I'm not delaying life — I'm living it. That watching sunsets from my balcony and spending three hours getting a shadow exactly right on a canvas isn't running away from responsibility. It's choosing what I find beautiful over what I'm supposed to find meaningful.

But try explaining that to a woman who raised four kids and never had a room of her own.

Nudge: "Do you ever want those things she's describing?"

Response: Sometimes. In the abstract. A house with a garden, someone to cook dinner with, kids running around. Then I think about what I'd have to give up to get there — the late nights, the freedom to take a trip on a whim, the quiet — and the wanting fades. Maybe it'll change. But I'm not going to build a life around a future version of myself that might never show up.

Nadia dragged me to a ceramics workshop today. I was reluctant — pottery isn't my medium, the class was expensive, I have work to do. But three hours in, covered in clay, laughing at my lopsided bowl, I remembered why I started making art in the first place. Not for galleries or commissions or validation. Because making things with your hands while talking to a friend is one of the best feelings in the world.

Came home and looked at the commission piece differently. Loosened up. Added a texture I wouldn't have tried yesterday. Sometimes stepping away from the work is the work.

Got an email from my uncle. The firm is expanding and he has a position that would be "perfect for someone with my skills." He cc'd my father. I haven't replied. I'm not going to reply. Three years of building something of my own doesn't disappear because the family found another door they want me to walk through.

Nudge: "What about the role itself bothers you?"

Response: Everything. Nothing. I don't know.

Can't sleep. Lying here thinking about money. The commission payment came through but half of it went to the credit card and the other half to the paint supplies and now I'm back to calculating whether I can make it to the 15th without another gig. This is the part they don't show in the artist documentaries — the 2am math, the spreadsheet of minimum payments, the way your stomach drops when you check your balance.

I keep thinking about my uncle's email. Not because I want the job — I don't — but because there's a version of my life where I said yes three years ago and I'm sitting in a nice apartment right now not worrying about rent. That version of me has savings. Goes on vacation. Buys things without checking if her card will decline.

But that version also doesn't have the coastal series. Doesn't have the crane sketches. Doesn't have the feeling I get when a piece finally works. Is the trade worth it? Most days I say yes without hesitation. Tonight, staring at the ceiling, I'm not sure. I think that's okay. I think doubt at 2am is just part of the deal.

A gallery in Abu Dhabi reached out. They saw the coastal series on Instagram and want to discuss a show in October. I screamed. Actually screamed, alone in my apartment, phone in hand. Then I called Nadia and we screamed together.

I know it might fall through. I know galleries ghost artists all the time. But right now, today, someone looked at the work I made in my cramped little studio and thought it was worth showing. That's enough. That's more than enough.

Booked a table at that Lebanese place we love for dinner tonight. The expensive one. My mother would say I should save the money. But this — this is worth celebrating. The work, the call, the possibility. All of it.

Got an email from my cousin Faris today, forwarding photos from his daughter's first birthday party back in Amman. The whole family was there — uncles, aunts, my mother holding the baby with that particular look she gets. I was not invited to skip it; I just was not there. No one said anything pointed but the group chat went quiet for a while after the photos dropped and I could feel the space where my name should have been.

Nothing happened, really. I ate leftover lentil soup, re-read a paper on morphological parsing, tried to focus. My flatmate Callum asked if I wanted to go to a pub quiz and I said no, then sat there wishing I had said yes.

Nudge: "What was the quiet in the group chat about?"

Response: It is not that they are angry. It is that they have stopped expecting me to show up. That is worse, I think. Like I have been reclassified from "absent" to "gone."

Supervisor meeting. Dr. Keane asked me to commit to one research question by next Friday and I heard myself say yes, absolutely, no problem. Walked home and could not stop thinking about the three other directions I had sketched out last month.

Nudge: "What stopped you from pushing back in the meeting?"

Response: Because pushing back feels like making a scene, and I do not make scenes. My father never raised his voice in a meeting in his life. But I keep wondering if the question I pick will be the one that bores me by October.

Library until six. Drafted the introduction for the narrowed thesis proposal, which took longer than it should have because I kept second-guessing paragraph transitions. Callum and I walked to the chippie on Clerk Street after.

Friday call with my mother went well for once. She asked about the thesis and I described the morphological analysis angle in simple terms — how computers learn to break Arabic words into their root pieces. She said it sounded useful, which from her is high praise.

I felt like myself on that call. Not performing, not deflecting her questions about when I am coming home. Just explaining something I actually care about to someone who wanted to listen. She even asked a follow-up about how the root system works differently in dialects. Small thing, but it settled something in me.

Second entry today because my brain will not shut off. After the call with my mother I went for a walk up Arthur's Seat. Halfway up I got this rush of clarity — or maybe it was just adrenaline — where I started thinking about what it would look like to pivot my thesis entirely. Not the careful narrowing Dr. Keane wants. Something bigger. Cross-linguistic transfer models for under-resourced dialects. Levantine, Gulf, Maghrebi. My heart was actually racing on the hill and it was not from the climb.

Then I sat on the bench at the top and the wind hit me and I thought about what pivoting would mean. Another meeting with Keane where I contradict what I said three days ago. My mother asking again when I will finish. My uncle Hisham, who helped fund the first year, shaking his head at another delay. Everyone back home already thinks this PhD was an impulsive detour, and changing direction again would prove them right.

So I walked back down, made tea, opened the narrowed proposal draft. Still sitting here with it. The cursor is blinking at the end of paragraph three and I have not typed anything in forty minutes.

Nudge: "What would happen if you brought the bigger idea to Keane?"

Department social last night at The Pear Tree. I went because Dr. Keane mentioned it would be good to show face, and I know what that means in academic circles — it means attendance is optional in the way that prayer is optional at a family gathering. So I went.

Ended up in a long conversation with Priya from the NLP group. She is working on code-switching detection in Hindi-English tweets and the way she talked about it — with this specific, focused energy — made me jealous. Not of the topic. Of the certainty. She has her question, her method, her timeline. She did not seem to need a detour.

I told her about the dialect transfer idea. She said it sounded fascinating and asked why I was not doing it. I said timing, funding, the usual. She shrugged and said "those are real constraints" in a way that felt like she was politely letting me off the hook. I appreciated it and hated it at the same time.

Nudge: "What did you want her to say instead?"

Response: Something like "do it anyway." Or "those are excuses." Someone outside the family orbit giving me permission to be reckless. Which is ridiculous because I am thirty-eight years old and should not need permission from a colleague I barely know.

Submitted the narrowed thesis proposal to Dr. Keane this morning. Forty-two pages, bibliography cleaned up, research questions scoped to morphological segmentation for Modern Standard Arabic. Felt like handing in a homework assignment I had done properly rather than one that kept me up at night, but there is something to be said for finishing.

Callum and I cooked mansaf tonight — or my version of it, which involves Greek yogurt because Edinburgh does not have decent jameed. He kept asking questions about the spices and I caught myself explaining things the way my mother does, with small corrections and zero patience. Made me laugh.

After dinner I sat with my laptop and, almost without thinking about it, opened a blank document and started outlining the dialect transfer idea. Not as a thesis. Just notes. No one has to see it. I wrote for an hour and it was the most alive I have felt in front of a screen in weeks.

My uncle Hisham called today. Not the usual Friday family call — a separate, pointed call. He asked how the PhD was going and I told him I submitted the proposal. He said good, asked about the timeline, and then said he hoped I was not "chasing new rabbits" — his phrase, not mine. Apparently my mother mentioned something about dialect research and he connected dots.

I told him everything was on track, the proposal was submitted, Dr. Keane was happy. All true. I did not mention the outline document from last night. It is not lying. It is managing information. People share what is relevant and I shared what was relevant.

Went to the Edinburgh Farmers' Market at Castle Terrace this morning, mostly for the za'atar bread from the Lebanese stall. Ran into Priya and her partner. We talked about the summer conference circuit and she mentioned a workshop on low-resource NLP in Copenhagen in September. She looked at me when she said it, like she knew.

I came home and emailed Dr. Keane asking for a meeting next week. Just to discuss, I wrote. I did not say what about. But I printed out the dialect transfer outline and put it in my bag. Maybe I will bring it, maybe I will not. It is in the bag though. That feels like enough for today.

Nudge: "Why print it out if you might not show it?"

Response: Because I need to know it is there. Like a door I have not walked through yet but I can see. If I leave it at home it is just a file on my laptop. In the bag it is something I chose to carry. I think I am going to show it. I think I have been working up to this for weeks and I just did not want to admit it because admitting it means it matters, and if it matters then Keane saying no will actually hurt.

Spent most of Sunday lesson planning for the week. Marcus's mom sent another email asking if I could provide extra worksheets for him to practice at home. I said yes before I even finished reading it. That makes four families now with custom homework requests.

Mom called during lunch. Same conversation we always have. She asked if I'm eating enough, if I'm sleeping. I told her I'm fine. She doesn't need to worry about me when she has her own stuff going on with Dad's knee surgery coming up.

Aiden finally raised his hand today. First time all year. He got the answer wrong, but I could tell he'd been thinking about it. I just smiled and asked a follow-up question that let him get partway there. Small moment, but I keep replaying it.

Nudge: "Why does that one stick with you?"

Response: He's been so withdrawn since his parents split up this summer. Seeing him try, even knowing he might be wrong—felt like he trusted the room enough to risk it. That's the whole point of what I'm trying to build in there.

Mrs. Patterson cornered me after pickup today. Spent twenty minutes telling me her daughter needs more challenging work and that the current curriculum isn't "stimulating enough." I nodded through the whole thing. Told her I'd look into differentiated assignments.

I wanted to tell her that her daughter is actually right at grade level and doing fine. That I have eight kids reading below benchmark who need my attention more. But I didn't. Just smiled and said I understood her concerns.

Now I'm drafting a custom enrichment packet at 9pm. For a kid who doesn't need it. Because her mom is loud.

Nudge: "Does that sit okay?"

Response: Not really. I keep telling myself it's just easier this way, that pushing back would make the rest of the year harder. But mostly I think I'm scared she'll go to Principal Davis. And I hate that I care more about avoiding that conversation than standing up for what I actually think is right for the classroom.

Staff meeting ran late. New reading assessment mandate for October. Everyone nodded along even though it means losing two instruction days. I didn't say anything either. Grabbed coffee with Lisa after—she's frustrated too but says there's no point fighting it.

The SEL committee met today and somehow I'm now also in charge of the parent workshop in November. I don't remember volunteering. Principal Davis just looked at me during the meeting and said "Sarah, you'd be great at this" and everyone turned and smiled and I heard myself saying sure.

I already run the book club. I already do morning bus duty twice a week. I don't know how to say no when everyone's looking at me like I'm the one who always says yes.

Called Mom after to vent and she just said "Sweetheart, you can't pour from an empty cup." Thanks, Mom. Very helpful.

Nudge: "What would happen if you'd said no?"

Response: I don't know. Probably nothing terrible. Someone else would've done it. But then they'd all be doing me a favor and I'd feel like I wasn't pulling my weight. It's fine. I'll figure it out.

Jaylen brought me a drawing today. Just a crayon picture of me and him and the classroom hamster. He said I'm his favorite teacher ever. Put it in my desk drawer with the others.

Some days that's enough. Today it was.

Sunday again. Grading. Always grading. Thinking about next week's parent conferences. Need to figure out how to tell the Millers their son might need an evaluation without them getting defensive. They're nice people, they just don't want to hear it.

Made pasta for dinner. Watched half a movie. Can't remember what it was called. Some romantic comedy. Fell asleep on the couch before it ended.

This is fine. This is just September. It always gets easier by October.

Parent conferences ran until 8pm. Smiled through all of them. Told every parent their kid was making progress, even when I had concerns, because who has the energy to have those conversations back-to-back for four hours?

The one I couldn't avoid was with Eli's dad. Had to tell him Eli's been struggling with focus and we might want to explore supports. He got quiet. Asked if I was saying something was wrong with his son. I backpedaled so fast. Said no, no, just thinking ahead, just wanting to help, never mind, he's doing great actually.

I know I should have held my ground. The kid needs help. But the look on his dad's face—I couldn't do it.

Nudge: "What stopped you from holding that line?"

Response: He looked scared. And hurt. And I just thought—this is his kid. His whole world. And I'm telling him something's wrong in a fluorescent classroom at 7pm on a Thursday. I couldn't be the one to make him feel worse. Even if staying quiet means Eli doesn't get the help he needs until someone else brings it up. That's on me now.

Caught up on emails. Planned next week's lessons. Went for a walk around the neighborhood. Nothing remarkable. Just a Saturday that felt like a Saturday for once.

Lisa texted about getting brunch tomorrow. Said yes. Probably the first social plan I've made in three weeks that isn't work-related.

Brunch with Lisa was good. We talked about school stuff but also about her sister's wedding and the trip she's planning for winter break. Normal friend things.

She asked how I was really doing. I gave the usual answer—busy, tired, fine. She just looked at me for a second and said "You know you don't have to be everything for everyone, right?"

I changed the subject. But I've been thinking about it all afternoon. I don't know how to be any other way. I don't know if I want to be, really. The kids need someone who shows up. The parents need someone who listens. Maybe that's just what teaching is. Maybe that's just what I am.

Nudge: "What did you change the subject to?"

Had a call with Sami from the Aqaba branch about the delayed container shipments. Third time this month. He kept circling around the issue without giving me a straight answer about what went wrong on their end. I told him I need the incident report by Thursday and he agreed, but I could tell he was just saying what I wanted to hear.

Spent most of the afternoon reviewing Q1 numbers with Dina. She's been solid this quarter — her team's turnaround times are the best in the division. Told her I'd put her forward for the efficiency award at the next leadership meeting. She seemed surprised, which is odd. She should know she's earned it.

Picked up the girls from piano. Layla has her recital next week and she's been anxious about it — keeps saying she'll mess up the second movement. Sat with her after dinner and ran through the piece twice. She nailed it both times. She's better than she thinks she is but she won't believe me when I tell her.

Nudge: "What's actually going on in Aqaba?"

Response: Honestly I think Sami's lost control of his warehouse team. Two of his senior guys left in January and he promoted people who weren't ready. But if I say that out loud it becomes my problem to fix, and I've got enough on my plate.

Second time opening this today. Just got off the phone with my mother. She wanted to know if Fadi is "settling in okay" which is her way of asking whether I'm watching out for him. He's fine. More than fine — he closed the Zarqa supplier deal on his own last week and didn't even tell anyone until the contract was signed. I called him into my office this morning to tell him I noticed. The look on his face. Like he didn't expect anyone in management to actually see what he's doing day to day.

That's the thing that gets me. I remember being twenty-seven at my first real job and my manager barely knew my name. I told myself if I ever ran a team I'd make sure people felt seen. Not in some corporate poster way but actually noticed. Fadi's doing good work and he should hear it from someone who matters in the building.

Noor texted me a photo of Rania's math test — ninety-eight percent. Already thinking about how to celebrate at dinner. Maybe we do kunafa from Abu Salem's.

Nudge: "Why does that matter so much to you?"

Response: Because nobody did it for me. I had to figure everything out alone when I started. My first manager couldn't even remember which project I was on. I just don't want that for people under me.
Got an email from HR about the VP role. They want me to "onboard" the new guy next week. Show him around, introduce him to the team leads. My team.

People keep asking me how I feel about the VP situation like they expect me to be bitter about it. I'm not bitter. The man hasn't started yet and I'm already being asked to hold his hand through orientation. Fine. I'll do it. I'll do it well because that's what professionals do and because everyone will be watching how I handle it.

Had the onboarding meeting today. His name is Khalil. MBA from London, five years at Maersk. He's polished. Asked good questions. I answered them thoroughly and introduced him to every team lead personally. Dina told me afterward she was impressed by how generous I was with my time. That word — generous. I don't know if that's what it was. I know how things work. The person who trains the new boss controls the narrative.

Fadi called me tonight to ask about a contract clause he wasn't sure about. Spent twenty minutes walking him through it. He thanked me three times. I told him to stop thanking me and just get it right.

Nudge: "Was it generous or strategic?"

Response: Both. I genuinely want the transition to go well for the company. But I'm not naive — if I'm the one who made Khalil's first week smooth, that positions me a certain way. I don't see anything wrong with that.
Friday at home. Noor's at the clinic for a half day so I took the girls to Teta's house. My mother made mansaf and spent the whole lunch telling Rania she needs to eat more.

Khalil scheduled a "division alignment" meeting for Monday without telling me first. Found out from Dina who got the calendar invite. She assumed I already knew. I didn't. It's a small thing but it's the kind of small thing that tells you how someone sees the org chart.

Had lunch with Amjad from finance. He mentioned casually that there are rumors about restructuring — possibly merging operations and logistics under Khalil. I kept my face neutral but inside something tightened. I've built this team from twelve people to forty-two. The processes, the KPI framework, the vendor relationships — those are mine.

Came home and Noor could tell something was off. She asked and I said work stuff, the usual. She didn't push. She knows when to give me room. After the girls went to bed I sat on the balcony for a while thinking about what I'd actually do if they folded my division. Started going through my LinkedIn for the first time in months. Not seriously. Just looking.

Nudge: "How serious is the LinkedIn thing really?"

Response: It's not serious yet. I'm not updating my profile or messaging anyone. But I want to know what's out there in case I need to move fast. I refuse to be caught off guard.

Fadi came to me today looking shaken. Khalil pulled him into a meeting and started questioning the Zarqa deal — asking why procurement didn't go through the new approval workflow that apparently Khalil set up without consulting me or my team. Fadi didn't know what to say. He did everything by the book, by the process that existed when he closed the deal.

I went to Khalil's office and told him directly that if he's changing procurement workflows he needs to loop in operations first because my people are the ones executing these contracts. He smiled and said he appreciated the feedback. Appreciated the feedback. Like I'm filing a suggestion at a hotel.

I'm angry but what's worse is I feel like I let Fadi down. He came to me because he trusts me and I brought him into this company. I should have anticipated that Khalil would start making changes without understanding the existing structure. I should have shielded him from this. Now Fadi's second-guessing himself over a deal he should be proud of.

Nudge: "Could you have actually prevented that?"

Response: Probably not. Khalil was going to assert himself no matter what. But I could have warned Fadi, prepared him for the possibility that new leadership means new rules. I just didn't think it would happen this fast.
Layla asked me at breakfast why I've been "grumpy." I told her I wasn't grumpy, just busy. But she's right. I've been bringing it home and that's not fair to them.

The restructuring rumor turned out to be exactly that — a rumor. CEO called me in on Thursday and told me operations stays independent, reporting to him, same as before. He also said he relies on my institutional knowledge and wants me on the quarterly strategy committee starting next month. I nodded, said thank you, kept it professional. Walked out of his office and called Noor immediately.

Took the whole family to Abu Salem's on Friday night to celebrate — didn't tell the girls why, just said we deserved it. Fadi texted me that evening asking for advice on a lease negotiation for his apartment. Nothing to do with work. I spent thirty minutes on the phone with him going through the clauses. Noor rolled her eyes but she gets it. Family is family, even at nine p.m. on a Friday.

Got back from the studio late and Baba was already asleep but Mama was waiting up. She didn't say anything about me being late, she just set out the leftover kibbeh and sat across from me while I ate. The whole time I could feel her watching me and I knew she wanted to ask about the commission meeting. I told her it went fine, which it did, mostly. The client wants something for his hotel lobby, large scale, blues and golds, very safe. I said I'd do it.

But the thing is I'd spent the whole morning working on something else entirely, this series I've been sketching for months. Faces in crowds, people looking away from each other. Disconnection in tight spaces. It's the most honest work I've done in years and I can't show it to anyone. Not because it's bad but because every time I imagine hanging it somewhere or putting it in a show I can already hear my uncle asking why I'm painting something so depressing, or my cousin making that face she makes when she doesn't understand something but doesn't want to say so. So instead I took the hotel lobby job and told myself the other work can wait. It can always wait.

I keep doing this. The safe thing first, then the real thing never.

Nudge: "You said the real thing never happens. What would have to change for it to?"

Response: I'd need to stop caring what people think, which is basically asking me to be a different person. Or I'd need someone to tell me it's okay, which is pathetic at my age.

Friday lunch was the usual production. My aunt brought her friend who has a daughter studying dentistry and somehow the conversation kept circling back to me and whether I'm seeing anyone. Mama smiled the whole time and said nothing, which is her way of agreeing without being the one to push. I just sat there eating and deflecting. "I'm focused on work right now." "Inshallah, when the time is right." All the correct answers.

My sister pulled me aside after and said I handled it well, which felt more like pity than praise. She knows I've been seeing someone casually, someone who isn't from the community, and she knows I'll never bring it up at Friday lunch. Not because I'm ashamed but because I can already map out the exact sequence of reactions and it's exhausting to even think about. My father asking quiet questions. My mother suddenly having a headache. My uncle making a joke that isn't a joke.

People think I'm easygoing because I never push back. The truth is I've just gotten very efficient at predicting which battles aren't worth starting. That's not the same thing as being at peace with it.

Nudge: "You said that's not the same as being at peace. What does the gap feel like?"

Response: Like holding your breath in a room full of people who think you're breathing normally. It's fine until it isn't.

Had a conversation with Rami at the studio today that's been sitting with me all evening. He got accepted into a group show in Beirut, contemporary stuff, political, boundary-pushing. They asked if I wanted to submit something too. I told him I'd think about it, which we both knew meant no.

He didn't push, which I appreciated, but later he said something offhand while cleaning his brushes. He said, "You're the most talented person I know who makes the least interesting work." He wasn't being cruel. He actually looked sad when he said it. I didn't know how to respond so I just laughed it off and changed the subject.

The thing is he's right. The hotel lobby commission is technically proficient and completely forgettable. The faces series sitting under the tarp in the corner of my studio is the best work I've ever done and no one has seen it. I keep telling myself I'm being strategic, building a reputation with the commercial work first, earning credibility. But at some point strategy becomes avoidance and I think I passed that line a while ago. I just don't know what to do about it because every option I can think of involves disappointing someone or drawing attention I'm not ready for.

Long day finishing preliminary sketches for the hotel commission. The client wants revisions already, more warmth, more welcoming. I nodded through the whole meeting and made notes. On the drive home I realized I'd been clenching my jaw for hours and my neck is completely locked up.

Mama called to remind me about my cousin's engagement party this weekend. I said I'd be there. Of course I'll be there. Everyone will be there. I'll wear the right shirt, say the right things, congratulate the right people. I'm good at this part.

Engagement party was fine. Loud. Lots of people I hadn't seen in months asking the usual things. How's the art going, meaning are you making money. Are you eating enough, meaning you look tired. When are you next, meaning why haven't you settled down. I smiled through all of it because that's what you do.

Danced with my mother at one point, which was actually nice. She was happy, genuinely, and I like seeing her like that. My father sat in the corner with the other men drinking coffee and nodding. He looked proud of my cousin. I wonder if he looks at me and feels something different, not disappointment exactly but maybe confusion. His son the painter. He's never said a negative word about it but he's also never asked to see my work. That silence says more than any criticism would.

After everyone left I helped stack chairs and my aunt squeezed my arm and told me I was such a good boy for always helping. I'm thirty-nine years old and I'm still being called a good boy. The worst part is I felt relieved by it. Like that's still the thing I'm chasing, the approval, the confirmation that I'm doing it right. Whatever "it" is.

Nudge: "You noticed feeling relieved at being called 'a good boy.' What does that tell you?"

Rami brought up the Beirut show again. He said the deadline was extended and there was still time to submit. I told him I appreciate him thinking of me but the timing doesn't work with the hotel commission. Which is technically true. But also I could find the time if I wanted to. I just don't want to deal with the aftermath of showing work that actually says something.

It's not cowardice. I know what it looks like from outside but it's more complicated than that. My family has a certain idea of what I do and who I am and disturbing that comes with a cost. Not a dramatic one, no one's going to disown me. It's subtler than that. It's the shift in how people look at you when you stop being predictable. I've seen it happen to other people and I've decided I'd rather keep things smooth. That's a choice, not a failure. At least that's what I keep telling myself.

Can't sleep. Spent the last two hours looking at Rami's work online, the pieces he's submitting to Beirut. They're raw and unfinished-looking on purpose and there's this energy in them that I used to have. I pulled out the faces series from under the tarp and laid them out on the studio floor and just stood there staring at them. They're good. I know they're good. That's not ego, that's honest assessment after twenty years of doing this.

I took a photo of the pieces laid out and almost posted it. My thumb was literally on the button. Then I imagined my mother scrolling past it and asking why the faces look so lonely, or my uncle forwarding it with a comment about how art these days has no beauty. So I put my phone down and covered everything back up.

I keep thinking about what Rami said. The most talented person he knows who makes the least interesting work. It's like a splinter I can't get out. If I showed the real work and people responded to it, everything would change. But if people in my life reacted badly, that would also change everything. I don't trust myself to handle that without losing something I need. The approval, the ease, the feeling of fitting in. People act like fitting in is small but when you've built your entire life around it, it becomes the foundation. I'm terrified of what happens if I pull a brick out.

Nudge: "What would pulling that brick out actually look like, concretely?"

Response: Submitting to a show, probably. Or just leaving the tarp off the canvases when my family visits the studio. Small things. Anyway, I need to sleep.

Something shifted today, or maybe it's been shifting for a while and I only noticed now. I was working on the hotel commission, painting this massive blue gradient that's supposed to evoke the sea, and I caught myself adding tiny figures along the bottom edge. Almost invisible unless you're close. Faces turned away from each other, like my other series. I painted over them before anyone could see but for a few minutes they were there and the piece was better for it.

My sister came by the studio to drop off food Mama had made. She lingered, looking at canvases against the wall. She pointed at one of the commercial pieces and said, "This is pretty." Then she pointed at the corner where the faces series was partially visible under the tarp and said, "What's that one?" I told her it was nothing, just experiments. She said it looked interesting and I should show her sometime.

That's the thing about my sister. She asks without pushing. If I ever show the real work to anyone in my family, it'll start with her. Not because she'll necessarily understand it but because she won't make me feel wrong for having made it. That counts for more than most people realize.

I drove home feeling lighter than I have in weeks. Nothing has actually changed. I still didn't submit to Beirut. But someone in my family expressing even mild curiosity about the work I actually care about made me feel less like I'm carrying this alone.

Delivered the hotel commission today. Client was thrilled. Shook my hand and said it was exactly what he envisioned, which I think is the most depressing compliment I've ever received.

Nudge: "Why does hearing 'exactly what I envisioned' feel depressing to you?"

Took my sister up on her offer. Showed her three of the faces pieces at the studio today. She looked at them for a long time without saying anything. Then she said, "These are different." I waited for the follow-up, the concern, the suggestion to maybe not show Baba. Instead she said, "You should make more of these."

I didn't know what to do with that so I made us tea and we sat on the studio floor talking about other things. But the pieces stayed uncovered the whole time. I didn't put the tarp back until after she left. Small thing. But I noticed it.

Nudge: "You noticed you left the tarp off. What do you think that means for you?"

Response: Maybe I'm getting tired of hiding the work. Or maybe I just needed one person to see it and not flinch. Either way the tarp felt heavier putting it back on than it used to.

Stayed late at the lab again. Got home after the kids were asleep. Dina didn't say anything but I could tell from the way she left my dinner on the counter instead of the table. That's her signal.

The thing is I know I should've been home earlier. Layla had her first swimming lesson and I told her this morning I'd try to make it. "Try" is doing a lot of work in that sentence. I didn't try. I got pulled into running another simulation batch and the hours just disappeared. And now my kid learned to float without me there and I can't get that back. I keep telling myself the PhD is for them, for our future, but some of these moments don't wait for dissertations to be finished.

Nudge: "Which moments are you most afraid of missing?"

Response: Honestly all of them. But the ones where they stop asking me to come. That's what scares me.

Monday again. Advisor meeting at 10, went fine I think, he wants the literature review chapter revised by Friday which is tight but manageable. Spent the afternoon in the lab calibrating the shake table sensors. Boring but necessary work, nobody else was going to do it.

Dina took Omar to the pediatrician because he's had this cough for two weeks. She texted me the update: nothing serious, probably allergies, they gave him a prescription. I should've gone with her but Mondays are impossible with the advisor meeting and the TA prep for Tuesday's section. She handled it fine. She always handles it fine. That's partly the problem I guess, she's so capable that I lean on her for everything outside the lab and at some point that stops being partnership and starts being something else.

Called my mother in the evening. She asked when I'm defending. When I said probably next spring she went quiet then started talking about my cousin Faisal who just got promoted to department head at the ministry back home. He's two years younger than me. She didn't say it outright but the comparison was loud. Told her I had to go because Omar needed a bath, which was true but also convenient.

Nudge: "What does it start being?"

Response: Just imbalance I think. Not worth spiraling about right now.

Second time writing today which is unusual. Just got back from putting the kids to bed. Layla wanted me to read to her and I did three chapters of that Junie B. Jones book she's obsessed with. Omar fell asleep on my lap halfway through. Small thing but I needed it.

Dina and I actually talked after. Not about logistics for once. She told me about this cooking class she wants to take on Saturdays, Palestinian dishes from her grandmother's recipes. Her eyes lit up describing it. I told her she absolutely should and I'd handle the kids Saturday mornings. She looked surprised, like she wasn't expecting me to just say yes. That bothered me a little because it means she's gotten used to me being unavailable.

But I said yes and I meant it. Saturday mornings are mine with the kids and hers for the class. This is something I can actually hold onto. The dissertation won't collapse because I take three hours on a Saturday. And Dina deserves to have something that's just for her, same way the PhD is my thing. Though calling the PhD "my thing" feels strange when it's supposed to be for all of us. Whatever, the point is tonight felt right. I was present and it didn't cost me anything I'll regret.

Pulled two all-nighters this week to get the chapter revision done. Submitted it at 4am. Body is wrecked. Dina asked if I was okay and I snapped at her, said I was fine when I obviously wasn't. Feel awful about that. She doesn't deserve my stress dumped on her.

Nudge: "What were you actually feeling when you snapped?"

Response: Guilty. Because she was being caring and I resented it somehow. Like her concern reminded me I'd been neglecting everything that wasn't the dissertation.

Apologized to Dina this morning properly. Not just a mumbled sorry but sat down with her during the kids' cartoons and told her I knew I was being unfair. She said she knows the pressure I'm under but she needs me to not take it out on her. Fair. Completely fair.

Made pancakes for everyone after. Layla put chocolate chips in hers in the shape of a smiley face. Omar ate his with his hands and got syrup everywhere. Normal family morning and I was actually there for it instead of hunched over my laptop. Advisor can wait until tomorrow.

New month. Advisor approved the revised chapter with minor edits. Felt good to get that confirmation after the crunch. Started outlining the methodology chapter, should be more straightforward since it's describing work I've already done.

Dina started her Saturday cooking class last week. She came home with maftoul and it was better than my mother's, which I will never say to my mother. The kids and I went to the park while she was gone. Taught Layla to pump on the swing by herself. Omar mostly ate sand. Standard Saturday. This arrangement is working and I want to protect it.

Lab mate Chen got accepted to present at the ASCE conference in November. Good for him. His advisor fast-tracked the submission. Mine hasn't even looked at my abstract draft from three weeks ago. I know I shouldn't compare timelines. Chen doesn't have kids, doesn't have a family depending on him, works weekends without guilt. Different situation entirely. But sitting in the lab watching him celebrate while I'm still grinding through Chapter 3 revisions, I couldn't help doing the math on how far behind I am.

Dina asked how my day was and I said fine. Didn't mention Chen. Didn't want to sound petty about it.

Nudge: "What does 'behind' mean to you here?"

Response: Just timeline stuff. Conference papers, publications. The CV metrics that actually matter for the job market.

Layla asked me tonight if I'm going to be a student forever. She wasn't being mean, just genuinely confused. Told her almost done. But the question rattled around in my head after. Am I taking too long? Am I dragging this out because finishing means facing what comes next?

Nudge: "What comes next that worries you?"

My mother called and dropped the news that my father's been having chest pains. He went to the doctor last week apparently and nobody told me until now. When I asked why, she said they didn't want to "worry me during my studies." I'm 38 years old and they're still treating me like I'm a kid who can't handle bad news.

I'm angry but I also know exactly why they did it. Because every time I talk to them I'm stressed about the PhD, about money, about the kids. So they stopped telling me things. They shielded me and now I feel like I failed them by being so consumed with my own problems that they couldn't even lean on me. Dina found me sitting in the kitchen at midnight staring at my phone. She just sat with me and didn't say anything for a while. Then she said we could look into flights for winter break if I wanted to visit. She's already thinking about logistics while I'm stuck in my own head.

I told my advisor I might need to go to Amman for a few weeks. He said it's fine as long as I keep writing. Of course that's what he said. Everyone needs something from me and I can't figure out how to be enough for all of them at once.

Spent most of the morning at the hospital with Mother again. Her blood pressure readings have been erratic and the doctors want to adjust her medication. I sat in that waiting room scrolling through my literature review notes, pretending I could focus on methodology while watching the clock. By the time we got home and I made sure she ate lunch and took her pills, half the day was gone.

I signed up for this PhD knowing it would be challenging. What I didn't account for was how often life would demand I put it aside. My advisor sent a gentle reminder about the chapter draft due next week. I haven't written a single new paragraph in twelve days.

Strange to be the oldest person in every seminar, still learning to say "I need more time" without feeling like a failure.

Nudge: "What does failure look like to you here?"

Response: I suppose it looks like proving everyone right — my former colleagues who raised eyebrows when I resigned, my brother who thinks this degree is a vanity project. They expected me to enjoy retirement properly: travel with my husband, babysit grandchildren, maybe volunteer at the temple. Instead I'm chasing citations at fifty-eight.

But if I'm honest, the deeper fear is wasting whatever sharp years I have left. There's a clock now that wasn't there at thirty. Every delay feels heavier.

My daughter called crying at eleven last night. Her husband is traveling for work again and the baby has been colicky for three days straight. She asked if I could come stay for the weekend to help. I said yes before she finished the sentence, and then hung up and stared at my calendar.

The weekend is when I was supposed to finally sit down and write. I had blocked off Saturday and Sunday entirely — no hospital visits, no groceries, no family dinners. Just me, my laptop, and the quiet of an empty apartment. I was looking forward to it the way you look forward to a small holiday.

Now I'm packing a bag instead. And I feel terrible for resenting it, even a little. She's my daughter. She's exhausted and alone and I'm her mother. What kind of person calculates their writing time when their child needs them?

But another part of me is tired of always being the one who drops everything. My husband won't go — he says he's "not good with babies," as if that's a permanent condition and not something you can simply choose to do. So it falls to me. It always falls to me.

I'm bringing my laptop. I'm telling myself I'll write at night after the baby sleeps. We both know that's not going to happen.

Nudge: "What would happen if you said no, just this once?"

Back from my daughter's. Wrote nothing. Slept maybe four hours each night. The baby finally settled on Sunday evening, just in time for me to catch the train home.

I keep thinking about the pork belly buns from that little shop near her apartment. We walked there Saturday afternoon while the baby napped in the carrier. Twenty minutes of sunshine and soft dough and my daughter laughing at something I said. Those moments felt like breathing.

Maybe that has to be enough for now.

Submitted the draft. Not my best work but it exists. My advisor responded within an hour with comments — mostly structural, nothing devastating.

Made congee for Mother tonight. She said it tasted like my grandmother's, which is the highest compliment she gives. Small victories.

Nudge: "How are you feeling now that it's submitted?"

Response: My cohort is organizing a hot pot dinner for next week. First time they've included me in something social — usually they forget the old lady in the corner. I'm looking forward to it more than I probably should. Haven't had good hot pot in months.

The hot pot was wonderful. Crowded restaurant, too loud to hear anything, steam fogging my glasses every time I reached for the lamb slices. Xiao-Wei sat next to me and we discovered we both grew up eating the same brand of sesame paste that nobody makes anymore.

Funny how food carries so much. My mother used to mix that paste into noodles on cold days. Now I'm in a room full of people young enough to be my children, fishing mushrooms out of boiling broth, and for two hours I wasn't thinking about deadlines or hospital appointments or whether I'm falling behind.

I ordered a second round of the taro dumplings even though nobody asked. Sometimes you have to be the one who says "let's keep going." They didn't seem to mind.

Walked home through the night market after. Bought a bag of pineapple cakes I definitely don't need. Didn't feel guilty about it even once.

Nudge: "This sounds different from the weekend at your daughter's."

Response: It is different. No one needed me to be anywhere else. No responsibility I was avoiding by being there. I was just having dinner because I wanted to, with people who expected nothing from me except to pass the chili oil.

Maybe that's the permission I've been missing — not from anyone else but from the situation itself. When there's no competing obligation, the enjoyment feels clean.

My brother called this morning to remind me that Mother's 80th birthday is in three weeks and "everyone is expecting a proper celebration." By everyone he means himself and the cousins who haven't visited her in months. By proper celebration he means I'll be the one booking the restaurant, organizing the guest list, and making sure the gift is tasteful enough to photograph.

I told him I'm in the middle of my comprehensive exams and he laughed. Actually laughed. Said something about how I could take a break from "playing student" for one day.

I hung up on him. First time I've ever done that. My hands were shaking.

I know I should call back and smooth things over. That's what I always do. But I'm so tired of being the one who manages his feelings while he dismisses mine.

Three hours later and I still haven't called him back. My husband thinks I'm overreacting. "He didn't mean it that way," he said. "You know how he is."

Yes. I know exactly how he is. I've known for fifty years. That's precisely the problem.

When I gave up my career advancement to take care of our father during his illness, my brother sent flowers. When I spent six months coordinating Mother's hip surgery and recovery while working full time, my brother called on weekends. When I finally, finally decided to do something entirely for myself — to pursue this degree I've wanted since I was thirty — he treats it like a hobby I can set aside.

I'm not even angry at him specifically anymore. I'm angry at the role I've been assigned in this family, the one I keep accepting because the alternative feels like abandoning people I love. The reliable one. The one who handles things. The one whose own aspirations come last because someone has to hold everything together.

My chest hurts. I should make tea and calm down but I don't want to calm down. I want to stay angry long enough to remember this feeling the next time I'm tempted to say yes to something I don't have room for.

Nudge: "What would it look like to not be the reliable one?"

Response: Terrifying. And also, maybe, free?

I think about my classmate Hui-Fang who went to Portugal last month just because she wanted to see the ocean. She didn't ask permission or coordinate schedules. She just booked a ticket and went.

I can't even imagine making a decision that clean. Every choice I make has to pass through a filter of who else it affects, who will be disappointed, who will need me to be somewhere else. I've been doing it so long I don't know if I'd recognize myself without it.

But there's something appealing about the idea of someone else handling Mother's birthday. Just this once. Just to see what happens.

Called my brother back. Told him I'd handle the restaurant booking but the guest list and gifts are his responsibility this year. He sputtered but eventually agreed.

Then I made myself a proper cup of oolong and sat on the balcony watching the city lights come on. It's cold but I wrapped myself in the blanket my daughter gave me and stayed out there until the tea was gone.

Small rebellion. Small pleasure. Both felt earned.

Comprehensive exams tomorrow. Studied at the library until closing, then walked the long way home through the park where the winter jasmine is blooming.

Stopped for beef noodle soup at my usual place. The owner remembered my order — extra green onion, light on the chili. There's something comforting about being known.

Feel as ready as I'll ever be.

Nudge: "What's going through your mind tonight?"

Passed. The committee seemed genuinely interested in my research, asked questions I hadn't anticipated but could answer because I've been living inside this topic for two years. Dr. Huang said my clinical experience brought a perspective the literature lacks. Coming from him, that means something.

Called my mother first. She asked if this meant I was finally finished and I had to explain that no, there's still the dissertation, probably two more years. She sighed and said at least I'd be a doctor eventually and she could tell her mahjong friends. I'll take it.

My daughter sent flowers. Pink lilies and a card that said "So proud of you, Mom." I cried in my office for a solid five minutes.

Tonight my husband is taking me to the French restaurant we've been saying we'll try for a year. White tablecloths, a tasting menu, the whole indulgence. I bought a new dress — dark green, silk blend, probably too expensive. I'm going to enjoy every course without thinking about calories or cost or whether I should be home checking on Mother.

For once, I'm just going to let myself have the moment.

This degree might be a vanity project. It might be impractical at my age. It might mean missing things, disappointing people, constantly negotiating between who I want to be and who everyone needs me to be.

But today, sitting in that conference room, defending ideas I've been developing for two years — I felt like the version of myself I imagined when I was twenty-five and too afraid to try. She showed up after all. Just took her a few decades.

Signed Arjun up for the Math Olympiad prep course at Kumon even though his regular tuition teacher said he wasn't ready. The prep costs $480 a month on top of his existing classes and I didn't tell Ramesh yet. Not because he'd say no -- he never says no to anything for the kids -- but because I don't want to explain why I pushed for it. The tuition teacher was careful about it, said Arjun is solid but not exceptional, that maybe we should wait a year. And I heard myself saying he'll rise to the challenge, that's how we've always done it, throw him in and watch him swim.

But the car ride home was quiet. Arjun was in the backseat reading some manga and he didn't ask a single question about what just happened. He doesn't know I enrolled him. I was sitting at the traffic light near Bukit Timah and I thought, am I doing this for him or am I doing this because Kavitha aunty's son got into the Olympiad last year and I spent three months hearing about it at every family gathering. Priya brought it up at the temple too. Her Rohan this, her Rohan that.

I used to be the one people talked about. CA at twenty-three. First in the family to work at a Big Four. Now the only scoreboard I have is my children's report cards and I read them like they're my own performance reviews. Knowing something is off and stopping it are two very different things.

Nudge: "What happens when Arjun finds out?"

Response: He'll do what he always does. Nod, pick up the workbook, sit at the dining table after dinner. He's obedient like that. Sometimes I wish he'd push back so I'd have a reason to question whether I'm pushing too hard. But he just absorbs it, and I keep adding.

Amma made pongal rice this morning without being asked. Just woke up early, did the tempering with the curry leaves and cashews the way she's done it for sixty years, and had it ready by the time the kids came down. Arjun ate three servings. Even Divya, who's been on some ridiculous diet she read about on TikTok, had a full plate. And for a moment the kitchen smelled exactly like my grandmother's house in Mylapore, that particular combination of ghee and mustard seeds and the brass vessels heating up.

I sat there and I was furious. Not at Amma. At myself. Because I can't make it taste the same. I've watched her a hundred times, written the recipe down twice, and mine is always missing something. She says it's the hand -- amma kai paakkum -- that the food knows who is cooking it. Which is the most unscientific thing anyone has ever said to me and also the truest thing I've heard in years.

She's seventy-eight. Her knees are bad and she can't stand for long anymore. She did this today because Arjun mentioned he missed her pongal rice last week and she heard him. She heard him and she got up at five in the morning for it. I need to learn it properly this time. Not from a recipe card. From standing next to her and doing it until my hands remember.

Spent the whole morning reorganizing the prayer room shelf because Amma said the Lakshmi lamp was in the wrong position. Then drove Divya to violin, Arjun to Kumon, picked up Sanjay's cricket whites from the tailor. Calendar is a Tetris board this week.

Nudge: "Was moving the lamp just another task or something else?"

Response: It needed to be done. Amma asked, I did it. Not everything has to mean something.

Ramesh asked why the credit card had a new recurring charge and I told him about Kumon. He just said okay, didn't even look up from his laptop. I don't know why I was bracing for a fight that was never going to happen. Maybe I wanted him to have an opinion about it.

Divya asked if she could skip the temple youth group meeting this Saturday for a friend's birthday party. I said no before I even thought about it. She went to her room and closed the door quietly, which was worse than slamming it.

Nudge: "Why was the quiet door worse?"

Response: Because slamming means she's angry and anger I can handle. Quiet means she's given up trying to negotiate. It means she already knows the answer before she asks and she's just going through the motions. I recognize it because I used to do the same thing with my own mother. Ask, get told no, retreat. I thought I'd be different but I'm running the same script.

Pongal committee meeting at the community centre. Finalized the vendor list and stage layout. Kumaresh uncle questioned my budget for the second year in a row and I had the spreadsheet ready this time.

Arjun's first Olympiad practice test came back. Forty-second percentile. His regular tuition teacher called to check in and I could hear the I-told-you-so in her voice even though she was perfectly polite about it.

Nudge: "What did you feel when you saw the score?"

Response: It's one test. Baseline. That's the whole point of a baseline -- you start somewhere.

Long week. Sanjay had a cricket tournament in Jurong, two matches on Saturday and one Sunday morning. I was there for all of them, sitting in the pavilion with the other parents, making small talk about school rankings and enrichment classes. The usual tournament of its own, except we're the ones competing and the kids are just the scorecards.

Amma had a fall in the bathroom on Sunday while we were out. Nothing broken, but she bruised her hip and now she's moving even slower. Ramesh wants to talk about getting a helper but Amma won't hear of it. Says a stranger in the kitchen will disrupt the house. She means disrupt her routines -- the morning puja, the way she arranges the spice boxes, the specific order she hangs clothes on the bamboo poles. I understand it. I just don't know how much longer we can pretend she doesn't need help.

Nudge: "Where do you land on the helper question?"

Response: Stuck. I agree with Ramesh practically but I can't be the one who tells Amma her way of running a house isn't sustainable anymore. She built this life, this order. Taking it apart feels like telling her she's done. And she'll look at me like I've betrayed something. I'm not ready for that look.

Divya played her violin recital piece for us after dinner and it was genuinely beautiful. Mendelssohn. She's been grinding at it for months and tonight she played it clean, start to finish, no stops. Sanjay actually put his phone down to listen. Amma was in her chair nodding along. Ramesh recorded the whole thing on his phone.

And when she finished I clapped and told her it was wonderful and she smiled -- real smile, not the one she uses to get through family photos -- and I felt my chest crack open a little. This girl who closes doors quietly and rolls her eyes at temple and fights me on every single thing she can. She stood there with the violin under her chin and she was radiant. I wanted to tell her that but I couldn't figure out how to say it without making it about her exam grade or her college application. So I just said "beautiful, kanna" and hoped it was enough.

Arjun's Olympiad score went up to sixty-first percentile on the latest test. He showed me himself this time, brought the paper to the kitchen counter while I was chopping onions. I could tell he was pleased but trying not to make a big thing of it. I said good, keep going, and he nodded and went back to his room. Simple exchange but it sat with me.

I keep thinking about what my father used to say when I brought home my marks -- he'd scan the paper, circle whatever I got wrong, and say "next time, full marks." No praise for what I'd already done. Just the gap. I told myself I wouldn't do that to my kids but I catch myself doing a version of it. Keep going. As if where he is right now doesn't count. As if the sixty-one is just a waypoint to wherever I've decided he should end up. He looked happy at the counter. I should have said that's really good, Arjun. I should have let the moment be what it was.

Nudge: "Could you still tell him?"

The office was quiet today, most of the team out for the holiday weekend. I stayed because there was a deployment window and someone needed to be there. Rima asked why I didn't just let the junior handle it. I told her it's not about trust, it's about making sure nothing breaks while the client's systems are live.

Seventeen years at this company. The chair in my office has molded to my shape. There's something comforting about that, even if it sounds pathetic written down. I know every hallway, every server room, every shortcut in the build system. That knowledge is worth something.

Nudge: "What makes you say it sounds pathetic?"

Response: Because my cousin Fadi started his own consultancy two years ago and everyone at family dinners talks about him like he discovered electricity. Nobody asks me about my work. I think staying in one place for this long makes people assume you had no other options.

Got pulled into a meeting about "organizational restructuring." They didn't say layoffs but the room felt like a dentist's waiting area. My manager kept using phrases like "right-sizing" and "strategic realignment." I sat there nodding, taking notes, asking no questions.

Afterwards I walked to the parking lot and just sat in the car for twenty minutes. Checked the bank app, checked the savings, did the math again. If they let me go tomorrow, we'd be fine for six months. Maybe seven if we cut the girls' tutoring. But that's not the point. The point is I shouldn't have to be doing this math.

Nudge: "You mentioned asking no questions. What would you have asked?"

Response: Whether my department is on the list. Whether seventeen years counts for anything. But you don't ask those things in front of everyone. You wait, you read the room, you figure it out without making yourself a target.

No news on the restructuring. Business as usual. Finished the API migration ahead of schedule. Had lunch with Marwan from the infrastructure team. He's been here twelve years and seemed calm about the whole thing. Said he's not worried because his brother-in-law works in HR. Must be nice.

Rima's mother is visiting next week. Started cleaning the guest room. The girls are complaining about having to be "presentable" all weekend. I told them it's three days, they'll survive.

Rima's mother made a comment about how I should "think bigger" and maybe start something of my own like Fadi. I smiled and poured her more coffee. What does she want me to say? That I'm keeping her daughter's life stable while her golden nephew burns through investor money?

Nudge: "What did Rima say when her mother said that?"

Response: Nothing. She changed the subject to the girls' school. Which is its own kind of answer, I suppose. She knows I don't want to hear it, so she steers away. I'm grateful for that, but also wondering what she actually thinks.

A recruiter reached out on LinkedIn. Startup in Dubai, fintech, "ground floor opportunity." The salary would be higher but it's a two-year contract with no guarantees after that. I looked at the job description for maybe fifteen minutes, then closed the tab.

Rima would say I should at least talk to them. My father would have said a bird in the hand. I keep thinking about what happens if the restructuring goes through and I turned down the one door that opened. But I also keep thinking about what happens if I jump and there's nothing underneath.

Nudge: "What would you need to know to feel okay about either choice?"

Response: Honestly, I don't think any amount of information would make me feel okay about it. Some decisions just feel wrong no matter which way you go.

Thursday already. Week went by fast. Shipped the authentication module, got a thumbs up from the client. The restructuring email came out finally, my department is safe. No cuts in engineering. Marwan was right, his brother-in-law probably knew the whole time.

Funny how relief doesn't feel like happiness. It feels like unclenching. Like you've been holding your jaw tight for weeks and suddenly you notice the ache. Took the girls out for ice cream after work. Layla asked why I was being "weird and nice." Teenagers are brutal.

Team offsite today. The new VP of Engineering flew in from the head office in Riyadh and gave a presentation about "innovation culture" and "embracing disruption." Half the slides had stock photos of people jumping off cliffs. I wanted to ask him if he's ever actually jumped off anything, but I kept my mouth shut and clapped when everyone else clapped.

During the breakout session, one of the younger engineers, Sara, pitched an idea to rewrite our core platform in a new framework. She was passionate, articulate, the VP loved it. I could see at least fifteen ways it would break our existing integrations. But the room was electric, everyone was nodding, and I wasn't going to be the old guy who kills momentum.

After the session, my manager asked me privately what I thought. I told him the migration would take eighteen months minimum and we'd lose two major clients during the transition. He nodded and said "that's why I need you in the room, Nabil." Which is supposed to be a compliment but felt more like being told my job is to be the brakes on everyone else's engine.

I don't want to be the person who always says no. But someone has to think about what we'd lose.

Nudge: "What would it look like to say what you think without being 'the brakes'?"

Had the annual review. "Exceeds expectations" across the board. My manager wrote that I'm "the backbone of the engineering team" and "a stabilizing presence during periods of change." I should feel good about this. I do feel good about it.

Rima asked what they said and I told her. She smiled and said "of course." Like it was obvious. Like there was never any doubt. And maybe that's the thing that bothers me — not that people expect me to be reliable, but that nobody finds it surprising.

Nudge: "What would you want them to find surprising?"

Response: I don't know. Maybe that I had an idea they didn't expect. That I pushed for something risky and it worked. But that's not me, and I'm not sure I actually want it to be. I just want the option to be seen differently, even if I never use it.

Quiet week. Wrapped up documentation for the Q3 release. Took the girls to their grandmother's on Saturday. Drove back along the coast road instead of the highway. Rima fell asleep in the passenger seat. Good drive.

Long shift today—twelve hours on the pediatric oncology floor. Lost a patient, a seven-year-old boy named Diego. His mother was inconsolable, and I stayed an extra hour just sitting with her, holding her hand because that's what my own mother would have done. That's what we do in my family. You don't leave people alone with grief.

I came home and lit a candle at my altar. Said a prayer for Diego, then another for his family. It felt right to do something, even if it's small. The other nurses on my floor, the American ones, they cope by going out for drinks or hitting the gym. That's fine for them. But I need the quiet. I need the ritual. It centers me.

Called Mami afterward. She didn't say much, just listened while I cried a little. Then she reminded me that this work is holy, that caring for the sick is what God asks of us. I don't know if I believe exactly what she believes anymore, but I believe in that feeling—that this job matters, that being present for people in their worst moments is something sacred.

I'm exhausted but I don't regret the extra hour. I'd do it again tomorrow.

Had coffee with my cousin Marisol after work. We talked about maybe getting an apartment together next year—it would cut costs and she's family, so I trust her. It's nice to have someone nearby who understands why I do things the way I do.

Nudge: "What would it mean for you to have Marisol closer in your daily life?"

Posadas season started this week. The hospital let me organize a small celebration for the pediatric patients who were well enough to participate. We made paper luminarias, sang "Pidiendo Posada," and I brought in tamales that my tía made. Some of the kids had never seen anything like it—they kept asking questions about why we go door to door, why we carry the figures of Mary and Joseph.

Explaining it to them made me realize how much I've been holding onto these things without really thinking about why. It's not just about religion, not really. It's about belonging to something bigger than myself. Something that connects me to my grandmother, my great-grandmother, and all the way back. When I sing those songs, I can hear my abuela's voice mixing with mine, even though she's thousands of miles away.

One of the other nurses, Jessica, said she thought it was "so cool" that I have all these customs. She said her family doesn't really do anything special for the holidays anymore. I felt sad for her. Not in a judgy way, just—I can't imagine not having that thread connecting me to where I come from. It would feel like floating.

I'm already planning what to bring for Nochebuena. The head nurse said I could have Christmas Eve off if I work Christmas Day, which is perfect because Christmas Eve is when my family has always gathered anyway.

My supervisor offered me a position on the travel nursing team. Better pay, opportunity to see different parts of the country. I told her I'd think about it but I already know my answer.

The money would help—I could send more home, save faster for the condo. But travel nursing means no consistent schedule, no knowing where I'll be month to month. My cousin is counting on me for the apartment plan. And I can't just leave Miami when my whole support system is here. Abuela wasn't thrilled when I left Guatemala; leaving again feels like I'd be proving something wrong.

Nudge: "It sounds like there might be more pulling at you here than just the practical decision. What feels hardest to say out loud?"

Response: The hardest thing to say is that part of me is curious. Part of me wonders what it would be like to just go somewhere new, not have to be the one everyone depends on for a minute. But that's selfish. My family gave up a lot so I could be here. I'm supposed to be building something stable, not chasing adventures. That's not who I am.

Spent Nochebuena with my cousins and their families. Marisol's mother made the tamales this year, and we all said they weren't as good as my tía's back home, but we ate them anyway and laughed about it. That's the thing about being far from home—you recreate what you can, and the imperfections become part of the new story.

Midnight mass was crowded. I sat between Marisol and her little brother, and when we sang "Noche de Paz," I closed my eyes and could almost pretend I was back in the cathedral in Guatemala City, the one with the blue walls and the golden altar. The incense smelled the same. Some things carry across distance.

Now it's New Year's Eve and I'm home early from a short shift. The city is loud with fireworks and parties, but I'm content to stay in. I cleaned my apartment, changed the water for my santos, and made a list of things I want to accomplish next year: get the condo, help Marisol settle in, call home more often, maybe learn to make mole the way my grandmother does.

It's strange to think about the year ending. I feel like I'm exactly where I should be—maybe not where I dreamed as a teenager, but where I'm meant to be. There's comfort in that. Knowing that I'm building something solid, something that will last.

Started the new year by setting up automatic transfers to my savings account. It's not much, but seeing that number grow a little each month makes me feel like I'm actually getting somewhere. Small steps.

Nudge: "Where is 'somewhere' for you? What does getting there look like?"

Had an argument with my coworker Rachel today. She made a comment about how I'm "always on the phone with my mom" and said it in a way that felt like she was making fun of me. I told her that in my family, we actually talk to each other, and she got quiet after that. Maybe I was too harsh but I don't regret it.

I know Americans have different relationships with their families. I know not everyone has a mother they can call whenever they need to hear a familiar voice. But the way she said it—like I'm some kind of child who can't function without parental approval—that stung. I'm not asking for permission when I call Mami. I'm asking for connection. There's a difference.

And yes, maybe I do ask her opinion on things more than Rachel asks her mother. So what? My mother has lived through things Rachel can't imagine. She raised four children while my father worked two jobs. She kept our family together when half the neighborhood was falling apart. Her opinion matters to me because she's earned it.

I'm not going to change who I am to make Rachel comfortable. If she thinks I'm too close to my family, that's her problem. I'd rather be "too close" than whatever she has going on where she barely speaks to her parents once a month.

Nudge: "Underneath the frustration, what did Rachel's comment touch in you?"

Response: It touched the part of me that sometimes wonders if I'm holding on too tight. If maybe the way I was raised doesn't fit perfectly here. I don't want to question how my family does things—we've been this way for generations and it's gotten us through everything. But sometimes I feel caught between two worlds, and comments like Rachel's just remind me that I don't fully belong in either one.

The condo I was looking at fell through. Someone else made a cash offer and the seller took it. Marisol said we could just find another one, but she doesn't get it. That was the building where our other cousins live. That was the whole point.

Everyone keeps telling me it's fine, that there will be other places, but I had planned around this. I had a whole picture in my head: Marisol and me in our place, walking distance from Lucia and her kids, Sunday dinners that don't require an hour of driving. Now I have to start over and I don't even know what I'm looking for anymore.

I talked to Abuela today. Really talked—not just the usual check-in where we say we're fine and miss each other. I told her about the condo, about feeling stuck, about Rachel and the travel nursing offer and all of it. She listened for a long time without interrupting, which is unusual for her.

Then she told me a story I'd never heard. About when she was young, maybe a little younger than me, and she moved from her village to Guatemala City for work. She said she cried every night for months. She said the city people made fun of how she talked, how she dressed, the food she cooked. She said she thought about going home so many times she lost count.

But she stayed. She stayed because she could see something on the other side of the hard part—a life she could build, children she could raise in a place with more opportunities. She said the crying stopped eventually. Not because it got easier, but because she grew into the person who could handle it.

"Mija," she said, "you're already that person. You just don't see it yet."

I cried after we hung up. Good tears, though. The kind that clean something out. I don't know if I'll find a condo in that building or if Marisol and I will end up somewhere else entirely. I don't know if I'll ever stop feeling caught between here and home. But I know that whatever I build, it will have roots. It will have her in it, and Mami, and everyone who came before.

That's enough. That has to be enough.

Nudge: "What would it look like if it were simply 'enough'—without the 'has to be'?"

Response: I think it would look like trusting that I'm not going to lose anything by being here. That keeping our ways alive doesn't require living in the same building as my cousins or calling Mami every single day. That the roots can stretch without breaking. I'm not there yet. But maybe I'm closer than I was this morning.

People keep telling me I take on too much. The vice principal said it again today after I stayed past six finishing the mid-term analysis spreadsheets.
