But someone has to track which students are falling behind, and nobody else is doing it systematically. It's not like I'm trying to be a martyr. I just know what happens when these kids slip through the cracks -- they end up in the same cycle their parents were stuck in.

Anyway, the reading club had its best turnout this week. Fourteen students. Two of them are reading a full grade level above where they started in September. That's not nothing. That's the whole point.

Nudge: "You say it's not martyrdom. What is it then?"

Response: I think it's that I can't separate myself from the outcome. Like if Ravi in 8B fails his English paper, I take it personally because I saw the warning signs in October and I had the data. If I'd just done one more review session, maybe. I know that's not entirely rational but that's how it sits in my chest.

Got the district assessment results back. My class average is fourth out of nine sections. Fourth. I worked harder than anyone in that staffroom and fourth is what I get.

Nudge: "Fourth out of nine is above the middle. What number would have felt okay?"

Response: First. Or at least second. I know how that sounds. But I mapped every student's weak areas, I differentiated the worksheets, I came in on Saturdays. The sections that beat me have teachers who've been doing this for fifteen years. I've been at it for three. Part of me knows I should be proud but the other part just sees the gap.

Still thinking about the rankings. Came home and opened my laptop to look at the breakdown by question type and I can see exactly where my students lost marks -- the inference questions, the ones that require them to read between lines. And honestly that tracks because half my class comes from homes where there aren't books, where nobody reads to them, where the language of the test isn't even the language they think in. So the test is measuring something, sure, but it's measuring something that has more to do with privilege than with whether I taught well.

Which makes it worse in a way because now I'm angry at the system and at myself simultaneously. Angry at the system for pretending this is a level field. Angry at myself for caring about the ranking anyway when I know it's flawed. I spent two hours building supplementary inference exercises tonight instead of eating dinner. Amma would lose her mind if she knew.

I keep circling. If I focus on the ranking I feel like I failed. If I focus on the injustice I feel helpless. There's no version of this where I just sit with it and feel fine.

Had the NGO water-mapping session today and it hit different this time. We tested samples from a borewell near the school and the iron content was nearly three times the safe limit. Three times. These kids drink this water. They fill bottles from this tap during lunch break. I've watched them do it.

I reported it to the block office last month and the response was basically a form letter saying it's been "noted for review." Noted for review. Meanwhile Lakshmi in 7A has been complaining about stomach aches every other week and I don't have proof it's the water but I also can't not think about it.

The grounding part is that at least now I have data. Six months of readings from twelve sites. The NGO coordinator said we have enough to go to the press if the block office keeps stalling. It feels like something I can actually push on, unlike the test scores where I'm just one teacher against a whole structure. Small difference, maybe, but it's mine.

Sunday and I told myself I'd take the day off but here I am at the kitchen table with three stacks of papers. One is the inference worksheets I'm piloting Monday. One is the water quality report I'm formatting for the NGO. One is the mid-year self-evaluation the school wants by Wednesday.

Amma called while I was in the middle of all this and asked when I'm coming home to visit. I said maybe February. She said I said that in November too. She's right. I haven't been back to Thrissur since Onam. Seven months. My cousin got engaged and I sent a gift instead of showing up. I keep telling myself I'll go once things settle down but things never settle down because I keep finding more things that need doing.

The self-evaluation is funny because they want me to rate myself on a scale of one to five across these categories and I genuinely don't know what to put. On effort I'd say five without hesitation. On results -- by the district's measure -- maybe a three? And then there's this whole other dimension they don't even ask about, like the fact that Ravi can now write a paragraph without freezing up, or that the water report exists at all. None of that fits in a checkbox.

Nudge: "Seven months away from home. What do you miss most?"

Response: My grandmother's rice kanji in the morning. The sound of the rain on the tin roof extension my uncle built. Amma not having to ask me through a phone screen if I'm eating properly. I miss being in a place where nobody's evaluating me and I'm not evaluating myself. I just exist there. But then I think about Monday's lesson plan and the water report deadline and the missing feels like something I can't afford right now.

Colleague asked me at lunch if I'd considered just "letting the NGO stuff go for a bit" since the term is getting intense. She meant well, I think, but it rubbed me the wrong way. Letting it go means those water readings sit in a folder and nothing happens. Letting it go means the block office gets to keep ignoring it. That's not something I can just put on pause because my marking load went up.

But I will say this -- I did manage to set a boundary this week. Told the NGO coordinator I can only do fieldwork every other Saturday instead of every week. It felt wrong at first, like I was abandoning something, but I also got through the week without that Sunday-night dread for the first time in a while. That's worth something.

Nudge: "You felt relief cutting back but also guilt. Which one do you trust more?"

Barely functioning today. Slept maybe four hours because I was up redoing the inference module after I realized the reading passages I'd chosen were too culturally specific -- references to summer camps and snow days that my students have zero frame of reference for. So I rewrote three of them using contexts they'd actually recognize. Market scenes, monsoon flooding, cricket matches. It took until 2 AM but when I tested the first passage on a few students during break today, the difference was immediate. They were actually engaging with the inference questions instead of getting stuck on vocabulary.

I'm exhausted but I'm also weirdly satisfied. This is what the district rankings don't capture. The test uses standardized passages that assume a particular kind of childhood. I can't change the test but I can build the bridge to it. My students scored low on inference not because they can't infer -- they do it constantly in their daily lives, navigating languages, reading social cues -- but because the test's version of inference lives in a world they've never been to.

I need to sleep more. I know that. But nights like last night are also when I do my best thinking because nobody's pinging me and I can just sit with the material. It's a trade-off I keep making and I'm not sure it's sustainable but right now it's working.

Three things happened today worth noting.

First, the block education officer called about the water report. They're sending someone to re-test the borewell next week. Not a full commitment to fixing anything, but the fact that they called instead of sending another form letter means the pressure is working. The NGO coordinator wants to submit the full dataset to the state environmental board as a parallel track. I agreed.

Second, I ran the revised inference module in two of my sections. Pass rate on the practice set went from 38% to 61%. That's with the same students, same difficulty level, just different cultural framing in the passages. I documented everything -- the original scores, the revised passages, the new scores. If I'm going to argue that the standardized test is biased, I need evidence, not just frustration.

Third, the vice principal flagged me for the district's "Emerging Educator" recognition. I didn't ask for it. Apparently my mid-term data tracking caught someone's attention higher up. I should feel good about it but mostly I feel like the recognition is for the wrong thing. They noticed the spreadsheet, not the Saturday reading club. They noticed the data, not the kids.

Amma called again. She's upset I'm not coming for Pongal. I tried to explain that February is when I need to prep the students for boards and I genuinely cannot take three days off right now. She said "you always have a reason" and I could hear that specific disappointment in her voice, the kind that doesn't argue, just goes quiet.

I don't know how to make her understand that the reason I can't come home is the same reason she wanted me to become a teacher in the first place. She raised me to care about this. She's the one who drove me to the library every Saturday when I was ten. She's the one who cried when I got the appointment letter. So how is it that doing the thing she was proud of is also the thing that keeps me away from her? I know she doesn't see it that way. She just misses me. But I can't hold both of those things at the same time without something cracking.

Week in review. The borewell got re-tested by the block office team. Results confirmed what our data showed -- iron at 2.8 times the limit, plus elevated nitrates we hadn't caught with our field kit. They've issued a notice to the school to install a filter system within 60 days. It took six months of volunteer hours to get to this point. Six months for something that should have been caught by routine inspection.

On the school side, I submitted my Emerging Educator portfolio. Included the inference module data alongside the standard metrics. I wrote a one-page note arguing that student performance gains should be measured against where students started, not against an absolute benchmark that ignores context. Don't know if anyone will read it. But it's on record.

Personally, I'm in a strange place. I should feel like I'm winning. The water issue is moving. My students are improving. I got recognized by the district. But I missed Pongal. I haven't called my grandmother in three weeks. I ate dinner at my desk every night this week. There's this gap between the things I'm accomplishing and how I actually feel, and I don't know what to do with it. I keep performing well by every external measure while something internal stays unsorted.

Nudge: "That gap you mentioned -- what would it take to close it?"

Response: I honestly don't know. Maybe it's not something you close. Maybe it's just the cost of caring about things that are bigger than your own life. The water report matters more than my comfort. My students' futures matter more than my sleep schedule. I believe that. But believing it doesn't make the tiredness go away, and it doesn't make Amma's silence on the phone hurt less. I think what I need is for someone to tell me it's okay to be proud and depleted at the same time. That those aren't contradictions.

Spent most of Sunday lesson planning for the week. Marcus's mom sent another email asking if I could provide extra worksheets for him to practice at home. I said yes before I even finished reading it. That makes four families now with custom homework requests.

Mom called during lunch. Same conversation we always have. She asked if I'm eating enough, if I'm sleeping. I told her I'm fine. She doesn't need to worry about me when she has her own stuff going on with Dad's knee surgery coming up.

Aiden finally raised his hand today. First time all year. He got the answer wrong, but I could tell he'd been thinking about it. I just smiled and asked a follow-up question that let him get partway there. Small moment, but I keep replaying it.

Nudge: "Why does that one stick with you?"

Response: He's been so withdrawn since his parents split up this summer. Seeing him try, even knowing he might be wrong—felt like he trusted the room enough to risk it. That's the whole point of what I'm trying to build in there.

Mrs. Patterson cornered me after pickup today. Spent twenty minutes telling me her daughter needs more challenging work and that the current curriculum isn't "stimulating enough." I nodded through the whole thing. Told her I'd look into differentiated assignments.

I wanted to tell her that her daughter is actually right at grade level and doing fine. That I have eight kids reading below benchmark who need my attention more. But I didn't. Just smiled and said I understood her concerns.

Now I'm drafting a custom enrichment packet at 9pm. For a kid who doesn't need it. Because her mom is loud.

Nudge: "Does that sit okay?"

Response: Not really. I keep telling myself it's just easier this way, that pushing back would make the rest of the year harder. But mostly I think I'm scared she'll go to Principal Davis. And I hate that I care more about avoiding that conversation than standing up for what I actually think is right for the classroom.

Staff meeting ran late. New reading assessment mandate for October. Everyone nodded along even though it means losing two instruction days. I didn't say anything either. Grabbed coffee with Lisa after—she's frustrated too but says there's no point fighting it.

The SEL committee met today and somehow I'm now also in charge of the parent workshop in November. I don't remember volunteering. Principal Davis just looked at me during the meeting and said "Sarah, you'd be great at this" and everyone turned and smiled and I heard myself saying sure.

I already run the book club. I already do morning bus duty twice a week. I don't know how to say no when everyone's looking at me like I'm the one who always says yes.

Called Mom after to vent and she just said "Sweetheart, you can't pour from an empty cup." Thanks, Mom. Very helpful.

Nudge: "What would happen if you'd said no?"

Response: I don't know. Probably nothing terrible. Someone else would've done it. But then they'd all be doing me a favor and I'd feel like I wasn't pulling my weight. It's fine. I'll figure it out.

Jaylen brought me a drawing today. Just a crayon picture of me and him and the classroom hamster. He said I'm his favorite teacher ever. Put it in my desk drawer with the others.

Some days that's enough. Today it was.

Sunday again. Grading. Always grading. Thinking about next week's parent conferences. Need to figure out how to tell the Millers their son might need an evaluation without them getting defensive. They're nice people, they just don't want to hear it.

Made pasta for dinner. Watched half a movie. Can't remember what it was called. Some romantic comedy. Fell asleep on the couch before it ended.

This is fine. This is just September. It always gets easier by October.

Parent conferences ran until 8pm. Smiled through all of them. Told every parent their kid was making progress, even when I had concerns, because who has the energy to have those conversations back-to-back for four hours?

The one I couldn't avoid was with Eli's dad. Had to tell him Eli's been struggling with focus and we might want to explore supports. He got quiet. Asked if I was saying something was wrong with his son. I backpedaled so fast. Said no, no, just thinking ahead, just wanting to help, never mind, he's doing great actually.

I know I should have held my ground. The kid needs help. But the look on his dad's face—I couldn't do it.

Nudge: "What stopped you from holding that line?"

Response: He looked scared. And hurt. And I just thought—this is his kid. His whole world. And I'm telling him something's wrong in a fluorescent classroom at 7pm on a Thursday. I couldn't be the one to make him feel worse. Even if staying quiet means Eli doesn't get the help he needs until someone else brings it up. That's on me now.

Caught up on emails. Planned next week's lessons. Went for a walk around the neighborhood. Nothing remarkable. Just a Saturday that felt like a Saturday for once.

Lisa texted about getting brunch tomorrow. Said yes. Probably the first social plan I've made in three weeks that isn't work-related.

Brunch with Lisa was good. We talked about school stuff but also about her sister's wedding and the trip she's planning for winter break. Normal friend things.

She asked how I was really doing. I gave the usual answer—busy, tired, fine. She just looked at me for a second and said "You know you don't have to be everything for everyone, right?"

I changed the subject. But I've been thinking about it all afternoon. I don't know how to be any other way. I don't know if I want to be, really. The kids need someone who shows up. The parents need someone who listens. Maybe that's just what teaching is. Maybe that's just what I am.

Nudge: "What did you change the subject to?"

Noor brought up the London program again at dinner. I could feel my chest tighten but I just said we'd talk later and cleared the plates. Haven't been able to sleep since.

Nudge: "What part of her bringing it up hit you the hardest?"

Response: She'd already looked up flat prices. Decided without me.

Spent the morning making maamoul with Hana and Tariq. They came over early, flour everywhere, the kitchen smelled like the house used to when Samira was alive. Hana shaped hers exactly the way her mother did, pressing the mold with both palms. I watched her and something settled inside me, like proof that what I've kept going all these years still matters to them too.

Tariq asked if we could do this every Friday, not just during holidays. I said of course. Small thing, but it sat well with me the rest of the afternoon.

Nudge: "Why did Tariq's request matter so much?"

My brother Walid called and said I should let Noor go, that I'm being selfish. Easy for him to say from Dubai with his kids in boarding school. Nobody in this family understands that keeping people close isn't the same as holding them back.

Came back to this tonight because Walid's call kept eating at me. After I hung up I sat in the garden for a long time. The jasmine Samira planted is still growing along the back wall, fifteen years now. I thought about how she would have handled this. She was always braver about letting the children try things. I think she would have been scared too but she wouldn't have let the fear win.

I don't know if that changes what I'll say to Noor. But I realized the anger I felt toward Walid was really just the sound of my own doubt getting louder.

Nudge: "You said Samira wouldn't have let fear win. What's the fear, exactly?"

Response: That if she leaves, the rest follow. House falls apart.

Noor cooked mansaf tonight. The whole thing, from scratch, without asking for help. She set the platter on the table the way I always do, rice shaped just right. Felt like she was showing me something without saying it.

Still thinking about the mansaf. The thing that keeps coming back to me is that she learned all of it here, in this kitchen, from watching me. Which means even if she's somewhere else, she carries it. I don't know why that's so hard for me to trust.

My father never let any of us leave Amman until we were married. I accepted that. But I also remember the years I spent wondering what it would have been like to study abroad when I had the chance. I told myself it didn't matter because I had responsibilities. Maybe it didn't matter. Or maybe I just never forgave myself for not pushing harder, and now I'm doing the same thing to her.

Nudge: "Is this about Noor, or about your own regret?"

Response: Maybe I'm afraid her thriving proves I didn't need to sacrifice everything.

Eid preparations started today and the house was full. Hana brought her husband and the baby, Tariq came with sweets from that bakery on Rainbow Street, even Walid video-called from Dubai to wish us well. Noor was in the kitchen all morning helping me roll grape leaves. For a few hours it felt exactly right, all the noise, the food, children running through every room. This is what I stayed home for.

But then after everyone left and it was just Noor and me cleaning up, she said quietly that she'd been offered a partial scholarship. She wasn't asking permission, not exactly, more like she was letting me know it was real now. I didn't say anything. I just kept drying the dishes and stacking them slowly.

I know I need to talk to her. I know what I'm leaning toward. But saying it out loud makes it final in a way I'm not ready for. The house will be so quiet without her.

Told Noor this morning she should accept the scholarship. She cried. I cried. We sat at the kitchen table for a long time not saying much. She kept saying she'd come back every summer, and I told her she should come back when she wants to, not because she feels she has to.

Spent the rest of the day reorganizing the pantry because I needed to do something with my hands. Found a jar of Samira's pickled turnips behind everything, must be from Hana's last batch. Put it on the counter where I can see it. The house is the same but it already feels like it's shifting.

Nudge: "Why put that jar where you can see it?"

Noor's been researching London neighborhoods all week, asking me to help her compare rental listings. Funny how she still wants my opinion on every detail even though she's the one leaving. I caught myself actually enjoying it, looking up transport maps, checking which areas have halal shops nearby. Like I'm planning a trip I'll never take.

Tariq suggested we start a family group chat so Noor can send photos when she's there. I said fine but I'm not learning any new apps. He laughed. I think this is how it goes, you hold something tightly and then you open your hand and it doesn't disappear. It just changes shape. I'm not all the way there yet but I can see the outline of it.

Nudge: "What does that outline actually look like?"

Got an email from my cousin Faris today, forwarding photos from his daughter's first birthday party back in Amman. The whole family was there — uncles, aunts, my mother holding the baby with that particular look she gets. I was not invited to skip it; I just was not there. No one said anything pointed but the group chat went quiet for a while after the photos dropped and I could feel the space where my name should have been.

Nothing happened, really. I ate leftover lentil soup, re-read a paper on morphological parsing, tried to focus. My flatmate Callum asked if I wanted to go to a pub quiz and I said no, then sat there wishing I had said yes.

Nudge: "What was the quiet in the group chat about?"

Response: It is not that they are angry. It is that they have stopped expecting me to show up. That is worse, I think. Like I have been reclassified from "absent" to "gone."

Supervisor meeting. Dr. Keane asked me to commit to one research question by next Friday and I heard myself say yes, absolutely, no problem. Walked home and could not stop thinking about the three other directions I had sketched out last month.

Nudge: "What stopped you from pushing back in the meeting?"

Response: Because pushing back feels like making a scene, and I do not make scenes. My father never raised his voice in a meeting in his life. But I keep wondering if the question I pick will be the one that bores me by October.

Library until six. Drafted the introduction for the narrowed thesis proposal, which took longer than it should have because I kept second-guessing paragraph transitions. Callum and I walked to the chippie on Clerk Street after.

Friday call with my mother went well for once. She asked about the thesis and I described the morphological analysis angle in simple terms — how computers learn to break Arabic words into their root pieces. She said it sounded useful, which from her is high praise.

I felt like myself on that call. Not performing, not deflecting her questions about when I am coming home. Just explaining something I actually care about to someone who wanted to listen. She even asked a follow-up about how the root system works differently in dialects. Small thing, but it settled something in me.

Second entry today because my brain will not shut off. After the call with my mother I went for a walk up Arthur's Seat. Halfway up I got this rush of clarity — or maybe it was just adrenaline — where I started thinking about what it would look like to pivot my thesis entirely. Not the careful narrowing Dr. Keane wants. Something bigger. Cross-linguistic transfer models for under-resourced dialects. Levantine, Gulf, Maghrebi. My heart was actually racing on the hill and it was not from the climb.

Then I sat on the bench at the top and the wind hit me and I thought about what pivoting would mean. Another meeting with Keane where I contradict what I said three days ago. My mother asking again when I will finish. My uncle Hisham, who helped fund the first year, shaking his head at another delay. Everyone back home already thinks this PhD was an impulsive detour, and changing direction again would prove them right.

So I walked back down, made tea, opened the narrowed proposal draft. Still sitting here with it. The cursor is blinking at the end of paragraph three and I have not typed anything in forty minutes.

Nudge: "What would happen if you brought the bigger idea to Keane?"

Department social last night at The Pear Tree. I went because Dr. Keane mentioned it would be good to show face, and I know what that means in academic circles — it means attendance is optional in the way that prayer is optional at a family gathering. So I went.

Ended up in a long conversation with Priya from the NLP group. She is working on code-switching detection in Hindi-English tweets and the way she talked about it — with this specific, focused energy — made me jealous. Not of the topic. Of the certainty. She has her question, her method, her timeline. She did not seem to need a detour.

I told her about the dialect transfer idea. She said it sounded fascinating and asked why I was not doing it. I said timing, funding, the usual. She shrugged and said "those are real constraints" in a way that felt like she was politely letting me off the hook. I appreciated it and hated it at the same time.

Nudge: "What did you want her to say instead?"

Response: Something like "do it anyway." Or "those are excuses." Someone outside the family orbit giving me permission to be reckless. Which is ridiculous because I am thirty-eight years old and should not need permission from a colleague I barely know.

Submitted the narrowed thesis proposal to Dr. Keane this morning. Forty-two pages, bibliography cleaned up, research questions scoped to morphological segmentation for Modern Standard Arabic. Felt like handing in a homework assignment I had done properly rather than one that kept me up at night, but there is something to be said for finishing.

Callum and I cooked mansaf tonight — or my version of it, which involves Greek yogurt because Edinburgh does not have decent jameed. He kept asking questions about the spices and I caught myself explaining things the way my mother does, with small corrections and zero patience. Made me laugh.

After dinner I sat with my laptop and, almost without thinking about it, opened a blank document and started outlining the dialect transfer idea. Not as a thesis. Just notes. No one has to see it. I wrote for an hour and it was the most alive I have felt in front of a screen in weeks.

My uncle Hisham called today. Not the usual Friday family call — a separate, pointed call. He asked how the PhD was going and I told him I submitted the proposal. He said good, asked about the timeline, and then said he hoped I was not "chasing new rabbits" — his phrase, not mine. Apparently my mother mentioned something about dialect research and he connected dots.

I told him everything was on track, the proposal was submitted, Dr. Keane was happy. All true. I did not mention the outline document from last night. It is not lying. It is managing information. People share what is relevant and I shared what was relevant.

Went to the Edinburgh Farmers' Market at Castle Terrace this morning, mostly for the za'atar bread from the Lebanese stall. Ran into Priya and her partner. We talked about the summer conference circuit and she mentioned a workshop on low-resource NLP in Copenhagen in September. She looked at me when she said it, like she knew.

I came home and emailed Dr. Keane asking for a meeting next week. Just to discuss, I wrote. I did not say what about. But I printed out the dialect transfer outline and put it in my bag. Maybe I will bring it, maybe I will not. It is in the bag though. That feels like enough for today.

Nudge: "Why print it out if you might not show it?"

Response: Because I need to know it is there. Like a door I have not walked through yet but I can see. If I leave it at home it is just a file on my laptop. In the bag it is something I chose to carry. I think I am going to show it. I think I have been working up to this for weeks and I just did not want to admit it because admitting it means it matters, and if it matters then Keane saying no will actually hurt.

Monday again, back on the bike by six. The app was slow this morning, kept waiting near Baner for orders that didn't come. Sat under a neem tree scrolling through other riders' complaints on the WhatsApp group. Same story everywhere — fewer orders, more riders, the algorithm deciding who eats. Literally. One guy said he made eighty rupees in three hours. That's not even petrol money.

Got a decent run after ten though, four back-to-back deliveries to those new tech park offices. The watchman at one of them made me use the service entrance and wait in a corridor that smelled like paint thinner. The guy who ordered — maybe twenty-three, twenty-four — took the bag without looking at me and closed the door. I don't know why that one stuck with me today. It's not new. Maybe because Ananya asked me last night what I do all day and I didn't know how to make it sound like something.

Meera had chai ready when I got back. She asked how much I made. I said decent. It wasn't, but she's already worried about the school fees due next month and I didn't want to add to that. Amma was watching her serial in the other room. Normal evening. Felt like holding my breath all day and only exhaling when I parked the bike.

Nudge: "What would you have told Ananya if you had the words?"

Response: She's eight, yaar. She just wanted to know if I drive fast. Kids don't need the whole picture.

Sunday distribution went well. We had enough packets for everyone at the metro site this time. One of the workers, a guy from Bihar, asked if we could bring blankets next week because the rains are starting. I said I'd ask the group. Walking back to the auto I kept thinking about how he said it — quietly, like he'd practiced asking so it wouldn't sound like too much.

School fees notice came. Fourteen thousand for the term plus two thousand for the activity kit. Meera put it on the fridge without saying anything. I did the math in my head at dinner and came up short by about five thousand. Could pick up extra night deliveries but the roads after ten are bad in monsoon. Amma asked why I wasn't eating. I said I wasn't hungry.

Nudge: "What happens if you tell Meera the real number?"

Response: She already knows. She always knows. She put the notice on the fridge instead of handing it to me — that's her way of saying we need to talk about it without actually saying it. I just haven't figured out what to say back yet.

Spent the morning fixing a leaking tap for Mrs. Kulkarni in B-wing. She always pays on time and gives me chai and biscuits, which honestly I appreciate more than she probably knows. Her grandson was there, studying for some entrance exam, books spread across the entire dining table. She told me he wants to get into IIT. She said it the way people say it — like it would save the whole family. I nodded. My father used to say the same things about my engineering entrance.

Didn't crack the entrance, obviously. Ended up in logistics. Then the warehouse. Then this. It's not a failure exactly, it's just — the life that happened instead of the one that was planned. I don't think about it much anymore but watching that kid hunched over his chemistry textbook brought it back. The pressure. The samosa breaks. The night before results when I couldn't sleep.

Afternoon was all deliveries. Nine orders, decent tips on two. The app sent me to NIBM twice which is far but the restaurant there packs well so nothing spills. Got home, helped Ananya with her Hindi homework. She writes her matras wrong still but she's getting there. Meera made bhindi. Amma complained it was too oily. Standard evening. Sometimes standard is fine.

Saw a news clip about the metro construction workers' camp being cleared for the next phase. The group chat blew up. Where do they go now? We've been bringing food there for months. Vinod-bhai said we should write to the municipal corporation. I said yes but I also know how that goes. Letters disappear. Meanwhile people get displaced and nobody even reports it.

Nudge: "What would actually make a difference there?"

Response: I don't know, honestly. Sometimes I think the food packets are just us making ourselves feel useful while the real machinery rolls over everyone. But then I think about that guy from Bihar who asked for blankets and how he looked at us like we were the only ones who noticed him. Maybe that's the difference. Not fixing the system. Just not pretending the people in it are invisible.

Meera's brother called today. Offered me a warehouse job at his friend's logistics company in Chakan. Steady salary, PF, the works. Thirty-minute commute if traffic cooperates, which it never does. The catch — it's a night shift, eight PM to four AM, six days a week. Meera wants me to take it. Her face lit up when she told me, like she'd been waiting for a rope to appear.

I should be grateful and part of me is. But I keep thinking about the Sunday distributions. Night shift means I'd sleep through Saturday mornings which is when we prep the packets. And I'd basically never see Ananya on school days. She'd be asleep when I leave and at school when I wake up. Meera says we adjust. Amma says a man provides. Everyone has an answer except the one I actually need — how do I hold everything without dropping something.

Nudge: "What are you most afraid of dropping?"

Response: The school fees aren't going to pay themselves, no? That's not a philosophical question, that's just math.

Meera and I talked properly after Ananya went to bed. She asked why I'm hesitating about the Chakan job. I told her I need to think. She said I've been thinking for two days and what's there to think about — it's a salary, it's fixed, it's what we need. She's not wrong on the facts. She's never wrong on the facts. That's the frustrating part.

I tried to explain about the Sunday group, about the metro workers being displaced, about how I can't just stop showing up when things are getting worse for them. She looked at me the way she does when she thinks I'm being impractical. She said "those people have problems we can't solve, Arjun, we have our own." And I know that. I do. But there's something in me that can't accept that the answer to our problems is to stop caring about theirs.

Went out to the balcony after. Sat on the plastic chair and looked at the street for a while. Auto-rickshaws, dogs, a man selling corn on the corner. Normal Pune night. I kept turning it over — steady paycheck versus everything else I'd lose. Meera isn't asking me to give up anything, she'd say. She's asking me to be practical. But practical has a cost she doesn't see because she's not the one standing at that metro site watching people get erased.

Told Meera I'd do a trial week at Chakan. Compromise. She seemed relieved. I also told Vinod-bhai I'd still come for distributions when I could and he said they'd manage either way, no pressure. That stung a little. Like the whole operation could run fine without me. But maybe that's a good thing. Maybe I needed to hear that it's not all on my shoulders.

Nudge: "What did you want Vinod-bhai to say instead?"

Response: Nothing different. He said the right thing. I'm just being sensitive about it. Moving on.

First week at Chakan done. The work is fine. Scanning parcels, loading trucks, shift manager seems decent. The money is real — I could feel it when I looked at the first week's calculation. Meera was right about that. The PF deduction alone felt like a small wall being built between us and the next emergency.

But I haven't seen Ananya properly in six days. She drew me a picture of a butterfly and left it on my pillow. Meera told me she asked why Baba isn't home for dinner anymore. I read her a story before leaving last night — she was half asleep, kept closing her eyes while I read, and I rushed through the last page because I had to catch the bus. That felt wrong. Not just inconvenient. Wrong. Like I was choosing a payslip over being present for the small things that make up a childhood.

Sunday distribution happened without me. Vinod-bhai sent photos to the group. They managed. New volunteers even showed up. And the metro workers' camp — it's already half gone. Fenced off. The remaining families are squatting near the nullah now. I keep looking at those photos thinking I should be there, but instead I'm forty kilometers away counting boxes for someone else's profit. Meera says it'll get easier. Amma says this is what men do. I don't know what I say yet. I'm too tired to figure it out tonight.

Nudge: "Is this the wall you wanted to build?"

Response: No. But it's the one I can afford to build right now. The butterfly drawing is on the fridge next to the school fees notice. That's my whole life on one fridge door. I don't know how to make both sides of it happy. I just keep showing up and hoping the math eventually works out to something that feels like enough.

The office was quiet today, most of the team out for the holiday weekend. I stayed because there was a deployment window and someone needed to be there. Rima asked why I didn't just let the junior handle it. I told her it's not about trust, it's about making sure nothing breaks while the client's systems are live.

Seventeen years at this company. The chair in my office has molded to my shape. There's something comforting about that, even if it sounds pathetic written down. I know every hallway, every server room, every shortcut in the build system. That knowledge is worth something.

Nudge: "What makes you say it sounds pathetic?"

Response: Because my cousin Fadi started his own consultancy two years ago and everyone at family dinners talks about him like he discovered electricity. Nobody asks me about my work. I think staying in one place for this long makes people assume you had no other options.

Got pulled into a meeting about "organizational restructuring." They didn't say layoffs but the room felt like a dentist's waiting area. My manager kept using phrases like "right-sizing" and "strategic realignment." I sat there nodding, taking notes, asking no questions.

Afterwards I walked to the parking lot and just sat in the car for twenty minutes. Checked the bank app, checked the savings, did the math again. If they let me go tomorrow, we'd be fine for six months. Maybe seven if we cut the girls' tutoring. But that's not the point. The point is I shouldn't have to be doing this math.

Nudge: "You mentioned asking no questions. What would you have asked?"

Response: Whether my department is on the list. Whether seventeen years counts for anything. But you don't ask those things in front of everyone. You wait, you read the room, you figure it out without making yourself a target.

No news on the restructuring. Business as usual. Finished the API migration ahead of schedule. Had lunch with Marwan from the infrastructure team. He's been here twelve years and seemed calm about the whole thing. Said he's not worried because his brother-in-law works in HR. Must be nice.

Rima's mother is visiting next week. Started cleaning the guest room. The girls are complaining about having to be "presentable" all weekend. I told them it's three days, they'll survive.

Rima's mother made a comment about how I should "think bigger" and maybe start something of my own like Fadi. I smiled and poured her more coffee. What does she want me to say? That I'm keeping her daughter's life stable while her golden nephew burns through investor money?

Nudge: "What did Rima say when her mother said that?"

Response: Nothing. She changed the subject to the girls' school. Which is its own kind of answer, I suppose. She knows I don't want to hear it, so she steers away. I'm grateful for that, but also wondering what she actually thinks.

A recruiter reached out on LinkedIn. Startup in Dubai, fintech, "ground floor opportunity." The salary would be higher but it's a two-year contract with no guarantees after that. I looked at the job description for maybe fifteen minutes, then closed the tab.

Rima would say I should at least talk to them. My father would have said a bird in the hand. I keep thinking about what happens if the restructuring goes through and I turned down the one door that opened. But I also keep thinking about what happens if I jump and there's nothing underneath.

Nudge: "What would you need to know to feel okay about either choice?"

Response: Honestly, I don't think any amount of information would make me feel okay about it. Some decisions just feel wrong no matter which way you go.

Thursday already. Week went by fast. Shipped the authentication module, got a thumbs up from the client. The restructuring email came out finally, my department is safe. No cuts in engineering. Marwan was right, his brother-in-law probably knew the whole time.

Funny how relief doesn't feel like happiness. It feels like unclenching. Like you've been holding your jaw tight for weeks and suddenly you notice the ache. Took the girls out for ice cream after work. Layla asked why I was being "weird and nice." Teenagers are brutal.

Team offsite today. The new VP of Engineering flew in from the head office in Riyadh and gave a presentation about "innovation culture" and "embracing disruption." Half the slides had stock photos of people jumping off cliffs. I wanted to ask him if he's ever actually jumped off anything, but I kept my mouth shut and clapped when everyone else clapped.

During the breakout session, one of the younger engineers, Sara, pitched an idea to rewrite our core platform in a new framework. She was passionate, articulate, the VP loved it. I could see at least fifteen ways it would break our existing integrations. But the room was electric, everyone was nodding, and I wasn't going to be the old guy who kills momentum.

After the session, my manager asked me privately what I thought. I told him the migration would take eighteen months minimum and we'd lose two major clients during the transition. He nodded and said "that's why I need you in the room, Nabil." Which is supposed to be a compliment but felt more like being told my job is to be the brakes on everyone else's engine.

I don't want to be the person who always says no. But someone has to think about what we'd lose.

Nudge: "What would it look like to say what you think without being 'the brakes'?"

Had the annual review. "Exceeds expectations" across the board. My manager wrote that I'm "the backbone of the engineering team" and "a stabilizing presence during periods of change." I should feel good about this. I do feel good about it.

Rima asked what they said and I told her. She smiled and said "of course." Like it was obvious. Like there was never any doubt. And maybe that's the thing that bothers me — not that people expect me to be reliable, but that nobody finds it surprising.

Nudge: "What would you want them to find surprising?"

Response: I don't know. Maybe that I had an idea they didn't expect. That I pushed for something risky and it worked. But that's not me, and I'm not sure I actually want it to be. I just want the option to be seen differently, even if I never use it.

Quiet week. Wrapped up documentation for the Q3 release. Took the girls to their grandmother's on Saturday. Drove back along the coast road instead of the highway. Rima fell asleep in the passenger seat. Good drive.

Supervisor meeting went fine. She said the lit review draft is solid but needs tighter framing around the methodology gap. Called Mama after, she asked when I'm coming home. Same conversation every Tuesday.

Nudge: "What does 'same conversation' actually sound like?"

Response: She asks when I'll visit, I say after the semester, she sighs, I change the subject to Noor's day at the shop. It's a script at this point. Anyway, the lit review feedback was useful — I should focus on that.

Three nights of maybe four hours' sleep each and I still didn't finish the conference paper draft on time. I emailed my supervisor asking for two more days and she replied with a single line: "Let's discuss priorities in our next meeting." I've read that sentence fifteen times. It could mean nothing. It could mean she thinks I'm falling behind. Both feel equally possible and I can't stop cycling through it.

Noor called tonight about the shop — the main printer broke down again and the repair estimate is more than what I usually send in a month. I told her I'd figure it out. Didn't tell her I've been eating rice and lentils for two weeks straight to keep my own rent covered. She doesn't need that. She's handling the shop and Papa's physiotherapy appointments and I'm over here unable to finish a twenty-page paper.

The worst part is I know people in my cohort half my age are publishing faster. Ahmed from the lab already has two journal submissions this year. I'm not jealous of him specifically, it's more that I keep measuring myself against a timeline that was never mine to begin with. I chose to come here late. I keep forgetting that.

Nudge: "You said you'd figure it out for Noor. Have you actually figured it out?"

Supervisor meeting was actually good. She wasn't upset — she wanted to talk about narrowing my scope so the paper is submittable by July. She drew this diagram on the whiteboard, crossing off two whole sections I'd been agonizing over. Felt like someone opened a window.

Walked home through the park after and sat on the bench near the pond for a while. Called Mama from there. Told her about the meeting and she said "habibi, you always worry too much." She's right, probably. Noor texted later that she found a cheaper repair guy through a friend at the souk. Small thing but I felt my shoulders drop reading it.

End-of-term presentations today. I went third. My slides were clean, my data was organized, I rehearsed four times this morning. And then during Q&A, Professor Chen asked a question about my sampling methodology that I couldn't answer clearly. I fumbled through something about stratified approaches and he just nodded politely. That polite nod — I know what it means. It means the answer wasn't good enough.

The frustrating thing is I knew the answer. I'd written about it in section four of my draft. But standing there with twelve faces staring at me, half of them twenty-six years old and already sharper on their feet, my mind just locked. I sat down after and my hands were shaking slightly. Nobody noticed, I think.

Came home and couldn't eat dinner. Opened the spreadsheet where I track my targets — publication by August, comprehensive exam in October, teaching assistantship application due next week. Every single line has a yellow or red flag next to it. I used to run project teams of forty people. I built a bridge overpass in Zarqa. And now I can't get through a fifteen-minute Q&A without my voice going thin. I keep telling myself this is worth it, that the degree changes everything, but tonight I'm not sure what "everything" even refers to anymore.

Nudge: "When you first decided to come here, what was 'everything' supposed to look like?"

Response: I think I pictured myself walking across a stage and my mother watching from the front row. Papa too, before the stroke. The degree was supposed to prove that leaving the career and the salary wasn't reckless — that I could build something bigger than a bridge. But now most days it just feels like I traded one kind of pressure for another, except this one pays nothing and nobody at home understands what a comprehensive exam is.

Quiet week for once. Submitted the revised paper draft, supervisor said it reads much better with the narrower scope. No major feedback, just a few citation formatting things. I spent Saturday catching up on the teaching assistantship application — recommendation letters are in, statement is drafted, just need to polish the research summary section.

Called home and for once it was a normal conversation. Mama talked about the neighbors' daughter's engagement party. Noor complained about a supplier who keeps shorting her on paper stock. Nobody asked me when I'm coming back. It felt different. Lighter.

I realized I've been here almost three semesters and I still eat dinner alone most nights. There's a departmental social on Thursday that I keep meaning to attend but I always find a reason not to. Last time it was the paper deadline. Before that it was needing to call home during the time slot. I'm not lonely exactly — I talk to my family every day and I see my labmates — but there's a gap between being around people and actually having someone here who knows me beyond my research topic. It's not urgent. It's just something I notice when the work quiets down enough to notice anything.

Ahmed asked me at lunch if I'd thought about switching to a more "applied" research track since I have industry experience. He meant it helpfully, I'm sure. But the way he said it — like the theoretical work is too much for someone who came in mid-career — sat with me all afternoon. I told him I didn't leave a perfectly good job to do a watered-down version of what I already know. He looked surprised. Maybe I was too sharp.

But I'm tired of the assumption that because I'm older and came from practice, I should be doing something "practical." I'm here because I wanted the harder version. I wanted to understand the theory underneath the things I built for twenty years. That's not a consolation prize, it's a deliberate choice. People keep framing it like I'm catching up when I'm actually trying to go deeper.

The thing I didn't say to Ahmed is that his question hit exactly where I'm already uncertain. The comprehensive exam reading list is dense and some of the mathematical modeling chapters make me read the same page four times. I am keeping pace, but barely. And when someone who's been in academia since undergrad casually suggests I take the easier route, it confirms the thing I'm afraid of — that maybe the gap between where I started and where I need to be is wider than effort alone can close. I don't actually believe that. But I didn't need him planting the seed.

Nudge: "You said you don't believe the gap is too wide. But you also didn't push back on the thought."

Good day. Actually good. Spent the morning working through chapter nine of the modeling textbook — the section on finite element analysis that's been haunting me for weeks. Something clicked today, like the pieces rearranged themselves while I was sleeping. I worked through three practice problems without checking the solutions until the end. Got two out of three right. The third was a sign error, not a conceptual one. That matters to me even if it sounds small.

Went to the departmental social after all. Stood in the corner with a cup of tea for the first twenty minutes but then Priya from the structures lab came over and we ended up talking for an hour about her fieldwork in Gujarat. She studies soil liquefaction. I told her about the overpass project in Zarqa and she asked genuinely interested questions — not the polite academic kind, the kind where someone leans forward. It felt good to talk about the work I did before as something that adds to what I'm doing now rather than something I left behind.

Walked home and the air smelled like rain. Called Mama and told her about the social. She said "finally, you're making friends" in that tone she uses when she's been worried but doesn't want to say so. I laughed. She's not wrong. Noor sent a photo of the shop with a new sign she designed herself — clean lettering, the family name in green. I saved it to my phone's home screen.

Teaching assistantship results came in. I got it. Starting September, I'll be a TA for the second-year structures course. The extra stipend means I can send Noor more without skipping meals myself, which honestly matters more to me than the line on my CV.

Sat with that for a while this morning. Three semesters of tracking every deadline on that spreadsheet, color-coding my progress, comparing myself to people who started this path twenty years before me. And here's a concrete thing that says: you belong here, you can do this, someone looked at your file and said yes. It doesn't fix the comprehensive exam anxiety or make the reading list shorter. But it's something solid to stand on.

Called Mama tonight to tell her about the TA position. She cried. Then she told me Papa smiled when she repeated it to him. I don't know if that's true or if she said it for me, but I'll take it either way.

Nudge: "What would it change if it were true?"

Response: Probably nothing practical. He can't travel here, he can't come to a graduation. But it would mean he knows I'm not wasting this. That the years away and the money spent turned into something he can recognize. I think that's what I've been working toward underneath all the spreadsheets and deadlines — just him knowing.

Spent the whole evening comparing fixed deposits on three different bank sites while the kids fought over the remote. Anil says we should lock in the rate before it drops again. I know he is right but I kept thinking about that weekend trip to Alibaug we never took. We had the dates picked and everything.

Nudge: "What happened to the Alibaug trip?"

Response: We pushed it. First it was school fees, then Anil's mother needed new glasses, then he said let's wait till Diwali. Diwali came and went. The dates are still saved in my calendar like some kind of joke.

My sister Meera called and asked when we are finally going on a proper holiday. I told her we went to Lonavala in June. She laughed and said "that does not count, it was one night at your cousin's farmhouse." I do not need her opinion on how we spend our time or money.

Nudge: "Did it bother you because she was wrong, or because she wasn't?"

Took Aarav and Rhea for their dental checkups. No cavities, which is a relief because the last round cost us eleven thousand rupees. Came home, made dal and rice, helped Aarav with his Hindi homework. He is struggling with the matras again. Called the tuition teacher and she can take him Tuesdays and Thursdays for two thousand a month.

Anil came back late. I had kept his food covered on the counter. He ate while scrolling his phone and went straight to bed. I watched two episodes of my show and finished the leftover gulab jamun from Sunday. The house was quiet. It should have felt peaceful but it just felt flat.

My mother-in-law brought up the gold investment scheme again at lunch. She wants us to put money into one of those jeweller savings plans, says every family needs gold as a safety net. Anil looked at me like I was supposed to agree. I smiled and said we would think about it.

Later I told him I do not want more gold sitting in a locker I forget the code to. He said it is about long-term protection, not about wearing it. Fine. But we have insurance, we have the FD, we have his provident fund. At what point do we stop saving for some imagined disaster and use the money to actually live a little? He got quiet and changed the subject.

Nudge: "What would 'living a little' look like right now?"

Response: Nothing extravagant. Maybe booking that resort in Goa I bookmarked three months ago. Getting the good cheese from the fancy store without feeling guilty. Taking an auto instead of waiting twenty minutes for the bus with two cranky kids. Small things that do not require a committee meeting.

I had a full breakdown over a cake. Not even a real breakdown, just stood in the kitchen with my hands shaking after I realized I forgot to order Rhea's birthday cake. Her party is Saturday. I had the tab open on my phone for two weeks, kept meaning to customize it, kept getting pulled away. She wanted the one shaped like a castle with purple icing and her name in gold. Now the bakery says they need five days minimum and I have three.

Called four other bakeries. One can do it but it costs almost double. I said yes without even checking with Anil because I could not bear the thought of my daughter's face when there is no castle cake.

Then I sat on the kitchen floor and cried. Not because of the cake. Because of everything the cake represents. How my days dissolve into errands and appointments and packed lunches and I cannot hold onto a single thing for myself. I used to coordinate events for two hundred guests. I used to keep spreadsheets and timelines and vendor contacts organized across six months. Now I cannot order a cake on time.

Anil found the charge on the credit card and asked about it. I told him the original bakery fell through. He said okay, no problem. He did not ask why I was upset. I do not think he noticed.

The worst part is that I know the party will be fine. Rhea will love the cake. The kids will run around the flat screaming. The mothers will sit in the living room and talk about school admissions. And I will clean up afterwards and load the dishwasher and think about the next thing. That is what bothers me. Not that it went wrong. That even when it goes right, there is nothing in it for me.

Nudge: "You said 'nothing in it for me' — what would something for you look like?"

Response: I honestly do not know anymore. That scares me more than the money stuff. At least with money I know what the number should be. But when I try to picture what would make me feel like I got something out of a day, I come up blank. I used to know. I used to have answers. When did I stop?

Rhea's party went well. She wore her purple lehenga and ate cake with both hands. I took about sixty photos. At one point she grabbed my face and said "best mummy" and I felt it go straight through me.

After everyone left and the kids were asleep, I poured myself a glass of wine I had hidden behind the pickle jars. Sat on the balcony with my feet up. Pune was noisy below me — auto horns, someone's TV, a dog barking. I just sat there drinking slowly and not thinking about anything. Forty-five minutes of nothing. It was the best part of my week and it cost me two hundred rupees and zero planning.

I need more of that. Not wine specifically. Just moments that belong to me.

Long week. Aarav had a stomach bug Monday through Wednesday so I was home with him while Rhea went to school. Spent three days washing sheets, making khichdi, and checking his temperature. He is fine now, already back to leaving Lego pieces everywhere.

Anil's company announced a restructuring. Nothing definite yet but he has been tense. Came home Thursday and said we should hold off on any big expenses for a few months, just in case. I agreed because what else would I say. We ordered in biryani that night and watched a movie together on the couch. The biryani was from the place near the station, the one that uses too much oil but tastes right. Anil fell asleep halfway through the film. I finished it alone and cleaned the containers.

Nudge: "What else would you have wanted to say?"

Sunday. Took both kids to the park in the morning. Rhea did the monkey bars for the first time without help and I clapped so hard another parent looked at me. Aarav just wanted to sit on the bench and read his comic book, which is fine, he has always been like that.

Came home and looked at my recipe blog for the first time in weeks. I have a draft post about paneer tikka that I never finished. The photos are on my phone, the notes are in a notebook somewhere. I thought about sitting down to write it but then the pressure cooker started whistling and Rhea needed help with her project and the moment passed. My phone buzzed with a notification that my blog traffic dropped forty percent this month. I closed the app.

Nudge: "What does the blog mean to you these days?"

Response: It used to feel like proof I still had a brain. That I could make something and put it out there with my name on it. Now it just feels like another thing I am failing at. One more tab I keep meaning to get back to.

Renewed our health insurance today. Bumped up the coverage to ten lakhs because Anil's colleague had a surgery that wiped out his savings. Felt right to have that cushion even though the premium went up. Also renewed Aarav's accident cover since he is starting football classes next month.

Made butter chicken for dinner using my grandmother's recipe. The one with the kasuri methi at the end. Both kids asked for seconds. Anil said it was the best I have made in months, which I will take as a compliment and not an insult. After they went to bed, I sat down and actually finished the paneer tikka blog post. Published it at eleven PM. Nothing fancy, but it is out there. Felt good to hit that button.

Spent most of the morning at the hospital with Mother again. Her blood pressure readings have been erratic and the doctors want to adjust her medication. I sat in that waiting room scrolling through my literature review notes, pretending I could focus on methodology while watching the clock. By the time we got home and I made sure she ate lunch and took her pills, half the day was gone.

I signed up for this PhD knowing it would be challenging. What I didn't account for was how often life would demand I put it aside. My advisor sent a gentle reminder about the chapter draft due next week. I haven't written a single new paragraph in twelve days.

Strange to be the oldest person in every seminar, still learning to say "I need more time" without feeling like a failure.

Nudge: "What does failure look like to you here?"

Response: I suppose it looks like proving everyone right — my former colleagues who raised eyebrows when I resigned, my brother who thinks this degree is a vanity project. They expected me to enjoy retirement properly: travel with my husband, babysit grandchildren, maybe volunteer at the temple. Instead I'm chasing citations at fifty-eight.

But if I'm honest, the deeper fear is wasting whatever sharp years I have left. There's a clock now that wasn't there at thirty. Every delay feels heavier.

My daughter called crying at eleven last night. Her husband is traveling for work again and the baby has been colicky for three days straight. She asked if I could come stay for the weekend to help. I said yes before she finished the sentence, and then hung up and stared at my calendar.

The weekend is when I was supposed to finally sit down and write. I had blocked off Saturday and Sunday entirely — no hospital visits, no groceries, no family dinners. Just me, my laptop, and the quiet of an empty apartment. I was looking forward to it the way you look forward to a small holiday.

Now I'm packing a bag instead. And I feel terrible for resenting it, even a little. She's my daughter. She's exhausted and alone and I'm her mother. What kind of person calculates their writing time when their child needs them?

But another part of me is tired of always being the one who drops everything. My husband won't go — he says he's "not good with babies," as if that's a permanent condition and not something you can simply choose to do. So it falls to me. It always falls to me.

I'm bringing my laptop. I'm telling myself I'll write at night after the baby sleeps. We both know that's not going to happen.

Nudge: "What would happen if you said no, just this once?"

Back from my daughter's. Wrote nothing. Slept maybe four hours each night. The baby finally settled on Sunday evening, just in time for me to catch the train home.

I keep thinking about the pork belly buns from that little shop near her apartment. We walked there Saturday afternoon while the baby napped in the carrier. Twenty minutes of sunshine and soft dough and my daughter laughing at something I said. Those moments felt like breathing.

Maybe that has to be enough for now.

Submitted the draft. Not my best work but it exists. My advisor responded within an hour with comments — mostly structural, nothing devastating.

Made congee for Mother tonight. She said it tasted like my grandmother's, which is the highest compliment she gives. Small victories.

Nudge: "How are you feeling now that it's submitted?"

Response: My cohort is organizing a hot pot dinner for next week. First time they've included me in something social — usually they forget the old lady in the corner. I'm looking forward to it more than I probably should. Haven't had good hot pot in months.

The hot pot was wonderful. Crowded restaurant, too loud to hear anything, steam fogging my glasses every time I reached for the lamb slices. Xiao-Wei sat next to me and we discovered we both grew up eating the same brand of sesame paste that nobody makes anymore.

Funny how food carries so much. My mother used to mix that paste into noodles on cold days. Now I'm in a room full of people young enough to be my children, fishing mushrooms out of boiling broth, and for two hours I wasn't thinking about deadlines or hospital appointments or whether I'm falling behind.

I ordered a second round of the taro dumplings even though nobody asked. Sometimes you have to be the one who says "let's keep going." They didn't seem to mind.

Walked home through the night market after. Bought a bag of pineapple cakes I definitely don't need. Didn't feel guilty about it even once.

Nudge: "This sounds different from the weekend at your daughter's."

Response: It is different. No one needed me to be anywhere else. No responsibility I was avoiding by being there. I was just having dinner because I wanted to, with people who expected nothing from me except to pass the chili oil.

Maybe that's the permission I've been missing — not from anyone else but from the situation itself. When there's no competing obligation, the enjoyment feels clean.

My brother called this morning to remind me that Mother's 80th birthday is in three weeks and "everyone is expecting a proper celebration." By everyone he means himself and the cousins who haven't visited her in months. By proper celebration he means I'll be the one booking the restaurant, organizing the guest list, and making sure the gift is tasteful enough to photograph.

I told him I'm in the middle of my comprehensive exams and he laughed. Actually laughed. Said something about how I could take a break from "playing student" for one day.

I hung up on him. First time I've ever done that. My hands were shaking.

I know I should call back and smooth things over. That's what I always do. But I'm so tired of being the one who manages his feelings while he dismisses mine.

Three hours later and I still haven't called him back. My husband thinks I'm overreacting. "He didn't mean it that way," he said. "You know how he is."

Yes. I know exactly how he is. I've known for fifty years. That's precisely the problem.

When I gave up my career advancement to take care of our father during his illness, my brother sent flowers. When I spent six months coordinating Mother's hip surgery and recovery while working full time, my brother called on weekends. When I finally, finally decided to do something entirely for myself — to pursue this degree I've wanted since I was thirty — he treats it like a hobby I can set aside.

I'm not even angry at him specifically anymore. I'm angry at the role I've been assigned in this family, the one I keep accepting because the alternative feels like abandoning people I love. The reliable one. The one who handles things. The one whose own aspirations come last because someone has to hold everything together.

My chest hurts. I should make tea and calm down but I don't want to calm down. I want to stay angry long enough to remember this feeling the next time I'm tempted to say yes to something I don't have room for.

Nudge: "What would it look like to not be the reliable one?"

Response: Terrifying. And also, maybe, free?

I think about my classmate Hui-Fang who went to Portugal last month just because she wanted to see the ocean. She didn't ask permission or coordinate schedules. She just booked a ticket and went.

I can't even imagine making a decision that clean. Every choice I make has to pass through a filter of who else it affects, who will be disappointed, who will need me to be somewhere else. I've been doing it so long I don't know if I'd recognize myself without it.

But there's something appealing about the idea of someone else handling Mother's birthday. Just this once. Just to see what happens.

Called my brother back. Told him I'd handle the restaurant booking but the guest list and gifts are his responsibility this year. He sputtered but eventually agreed.

Then I made myself a proper cup of oolong and sat on the balcony watching the city lights come on. It's cold but I wrapped myself in the blanket my daughter gave me and stayed out there until the tea was gone.

Small rebellion. Small pleasure. Both felt earned.

Comprehensive exams tomorrow. Studied at the library until closing, then walked the long way home through the park where the winter jasmine is blooming.

Stopped for beef noodle soup at my usual place. The owner remembered my order — extra green onion, light on the chili. There's something comforting about being known.

Feel as ready as I'll ever be.

Nudge: "What's going through your mind tonight?"

Passed. The committee seemed genuinely interested in my research, asked questions I hadn't anticipated but could answer because I've been living inside this topic for two years. Dr. Huang said my clinical experience brought a perspective the literature lacks. Coming from him, that means something.

Called my mother first. She asked if this meant I was finally finished and I had to explain that no, there's still the dissertation, probably two more years. She sighed and said at least I'd be a doctor eventually and she could tell her mahjong friends. I'll take it.

My daughter sent flowers. Pink lilies and a card that said "So proud of you, Mom." I cried in my office for a solid five minutes.

Tonight my husband is taking me to the French restaurant we've been saying we'll try for a year. White tablecloths, a tasting menu, the whole indulgence. I bought a new dress — dark green, silk blend, probably too expensive. I'm going to enjoy every course without thinking about calories or cost or whether I should be home checking on Mother.

For once, I'm just going to let myself have the moment.

This degree might be a vanity project. It might be impractical at my age. It might mean missing things, disappointing people, constantly negotiating between who I want to be and who everyone needs me to be.

But today, sitting in that conference room, defending ideas I've been developing for two years — I felt like the version of myself I imagined when I was twenty-five and too afraid to try. She showed up after all. Just took her a few decades.

People keep telling me I take on too much. The vice principal said it again today after I stayed past six finishing the mid-term analysis spreadsheets. But someone has to track which students are falling behind, and nobody else is doing it systematically. It's not like I'm trying to be a martyr. I just know what happens when these kids slip through the cracks -- they end up in the same cycle their parents were stuck in.

Anyway, the reading club had its best turnout this week. Fourteen students. Two of them are reading a full grade level above where they started in September. That's not nothing. That's the whole point.

Nudge: "You say it's not martyrdom. What is it then?"

Response: I think it's that I can't separate myself from the outcome. Like if Ravi in 8B fails his English paper, I take it personally because I saw the warning signs in October and I had the data. If I'd just done one more review session, maybe. I know that's not entirely rational but that's how it sits in my chest.

Got the district assessment results back. My class average is fourth out of nine sections. Fourth. I worked harder than anyone in that staffroom and fourth is what I get.

Nudge: "Fourth out of nine is above the middle. What number would have felt okay?"

Response: First. Or at least second. I know how that sounds. But I mapped every student's weak areas, I differentiated the worksheets, I came in on Saturdays. The sections that beat me have teachers who've been doing this for fifteen years. I've been at it for three. Part of me knows I should be proud but the other part just sees the gap.

Still thinking about the rankings. Came home and opened my laptop to look at the breakdown by question type and I can see exactly where my students lost marks -- the inference questions, the ones that require them to read between lines. And honestly that tracks because half my class comes from homes where there aren't books, where nobody reads to them, where the language of the test isn't even the language they think in. So the test is measuring something, sure, but it's measuring something that has more to do with privilege than with whether I taught well.

Which makes it worse in a way because now I'm angry at the system and at myself simultaneously. Angry at the system for pretending this is a level field. Angry at myself for caring about the ranking anyway when I know it's flawed. I spent two hours building supplementary inference exercises tonight instead of eating dinner. Amma would lose her mind if she knew.

I keep circling. If I focus on the ranking I feel like I failed. If I focus on the injustice I feel helpless. There's no version of this where I just sit with it and feel fine.

Had the NGO water-mapping session today and it hit different this time. We tested samples from a borewell near the school and the iron content was nearly three times the safe limit. Three times. These kids drink this water. They fill bottles from this tap during lunch break. I've watched them do it.

I reported it to the block office last month and the response was basically a form letter saying it's been "noted for review." Noted for review. Meanwhile Lakshmi in 7A has been complaining about stomach aches every other week and I don't have proof it's the water but I also can't not think about it.

The grounding part is that at least now I have data. Six months of readings from twelve sites. The NGO coordinator said we have enough to go to the press if the block office keeps stalling. It feels like something I can actually push on, unlike the test scores where I'm just one teacher against a whole structure. Small difference, maybe, but it's mine.

Sunday and I told myself I'd take the day off but here I am at the kitchen table with three stacks of papers. One is the inference worksheets I'm piloting Monday. One is the water quality report I'm formatting for the NGO. One is the mid-year self-evaluation the school wants by Wednesday.

Amma called while I was in the middle of all this and asked when I'm coming home to visit. I said maybe February. She said I said that in November too. She's right. I haven't been back to Thrissur since Onam. Seven months. My cousin got engaged and I sent a gift instead of showing up. I keep telling myself I'll go once things settle down but things never settle down because I keep finding more things that need doing.

The self-evaluation is funny because they want me to rate myself on a scale of one to five across these categories and I genuinely don't know what to put. On effort I'd say five without hesitation. On results -- by the district's measure -- maybe a three? And then there's this whole other dimension they don't even ask about, like the fact that Ravi can now write a paragraph without freezing up, or that the water report exists at all. None of that fits in a checkbox.

Nudge: "Seven months away from home. What do you miss most?"

Response: My grandmother's rice kanji in the morning. The sound of the rain on the tin roof extension my uncle built. Amma not having to ask me through a phone screen if I'm eating properly. I miss being in a place where nobody's evaluating me and I'm not evaluating myself. I just exist there. But then I think about Monday's lesson plan and the water report deadline and the missing feels like something I can't afford right now.

Colleague asked me at lunch if I'd considered just "letting the NGO stuff go for a bit" since the term is getting intense. She meant well, I think, but it rubbed me the wrong way. Letting it go means those water readings sit in a folder and nothing happens. Letting it go means the block office gets to keep ignoring it. That's not something I can just put on pause because my marking load went up.

But I will say this -- I did manage to set a boundary this week. Told the NGO coordinator I can only do fieldwork every other Saturday instead of every week. It felt wrong at first, like I was abandoning something, but I also got through the week without that Sunday-night dread for the first time in a while. That's worth something.

Nudge: "You felt relief cutting back but also guilt. Which one do you trust more?"

Barely functioning today. Slept maybe four hours because I was up redoing the inference module after I realized the reading passages I'd chosen were too culturally specific -- references to summer camps and snow days that my students have zero frame of reference for. So I rewrote three of them using contexts they'd actually recognize. Market scenes, monsoon flooding, cricket matches. It took until 2 AM but when I tested the first passage on a few students during break today, the difference was immediate. They were actually engaging with the inference questions instead of getting stuck on vocabulary.

I'm exhausted but I'm also weirdly satisfied. This is what the district rankings don't capture. The test uses standardized passages that assume a particular kind of childhood. I can't change the test but I can build the bridge to it. My students scored low on inference not because they can't infer -- they do it constantly in their daily lives, navigating languages, reading social cues -- but because the test's version of inference lives in a world they've never been to.

I need to sleep more. I know that. But nights like last night are also when I do my best thinking because nobody's pinging me and I can just sit with the material. It's a trade-off I keep making and I'm not sure it's sustainable but right now it's working.

Three things happened today worth noting.

First, the block education officer called about the water report. They're sending someone to re-test the borewell next week. Not a full commitment to fixing anything, but the fact that they called instead of sending another form letter means the pressure is working.
