The NGO coordinator wants to submit the full dataset to the state environmental board as a parallel track. I agreed.

Second, I ran the revised inference module in two of my sections. Pass rate on the practice set went from 38% to 61%. That's with the same students, same difficulty level, just different cultural framing in the passages. I documented everything -- the original scores, the revised passages, the new scores. If I'm going to argue that the standardized test is biased, I need evidence, not just frustration.

Third, the vice principal flagged me for the district's "Emerging Educator" recognition. I didn't ask for it. Apparently my mid-term data tracking caught someone's attention higher up. I should feel good about it but mostly I feel like the recognition is for the wrong thing. They noticed the spreadsheet, not the Saturday reading club. They noticed the data, not the kids.

Amma called again. She's upset I'm not coming for Pongal. I tried to explain that February is when I need to prep the students for boards and I genuinely cannot take three days off right now. She said "you always have a reason" and I could hear that specific disappointment in her voice, the kind that doesn't argue, just goes quiet.

I don't know how to make her understand that the reason I can't come home is the same reason she wanted me to become a teacher in the first place. She raised me to care about this. She's the one who drove me to the library every Saturday when I was ten. She's the one who cried when I got the appointment letter. So how is it that doing the thing she was proud of is also the thing that keeps me away from her? I know she doesn't see it that way. She just misses me. But I can't hold both of those things at the same time without something cracking.

Week in review. The borewell got re-tested by the block office team. Results confirmed what our data showed -- iron at 2.8 times the limit, plus elevated nitrates we hadn't caught with our field kit. They've issued a notice to the school to install a filter system within 60 days. It took six months of volunteer hours to get to this point. Six months for something that should have been caught by routine inspection.

On the school side, I submitted my Emerging Educator portfolio. Included the inference module data alongside the standard metrics. I wrote a one-page note arguing that student performance gains should be measured against where students started, not against an absolute benchmark that ignores context. Don't know if anyone will read it. But it's on record.

Personally, I'm in a strange place. I should feel like I'm winning. The water issue is moving. My students are improving. I got recognized by the district. But I missed Pongal. I haven't called my grandmother in three weeks. I ate dinner at my desk every night this week. There's this gap between the things I'm accomplishing and how I actually feel, and I don't know what to do with it. I keep performing well by every external measure while something internal stays unsorted.

Nudge: "That gap you mentioned -- what would it take to close it?"

Response: I honestly don't know. Maybe it's not something you close. Maybe it's just the cost of caring about things that are bigger than your own life. The water report matters more than my comfort. My students' futures matter more than my sleep schedule. I believe that. But believing it doesn't make the tiredness go away, and it doesn't make Amma's silence on the phone hurt less. I think what I need is for someone to tell me it's okay to be proud and depleted at the same time. That those aren't contradictions.

Made the kheer for Amma's birthday exactly how Nani used to make it. The cardamom from the Indian shop on Belgrave Road, not the supermarket stuff. Sat there stirring for forty-five minutes, watching the milk thicken, and it felt right. Like the kitchen back home, like Nani's hands guiding mine when I was eight years old and barely tall enough to reach the stove.

Called Amma after. She cried a little when I described the smell. Said she could almost taste it through the phone. She asked when I was coming next, and I told her maybe Diwali if I can get the time off. She said she will save her sari for me to help her drape it, like I always do.

Nudge: "What is it about draping the sari together?"

Response: It is the only time I feel like her daughter again, not just someone who calls and sends money. She cannot do the pleats properly anymore, her hands shake. But she waits for me instead of asking the neighbour. It is our thing.

Double shift again because Meena called in sick. I do not mind covering, but by hour fourteen my feet were just throbbing and I kept thinking about how I had promised to video call Appa before his morning walk. He waits by the phone like a child waiting for sweets. I texted him instead, said I was still at work, and he just replied "okay beta" with that period at the end that always makes me feel like I have disappointed him even when he says nothing is wrong.

Reena stopped by the nurses' station around two in the morning with tea. She said I look tired, which is her way of saying I look like death. I laughed it off, but she is right. I have been covering too many shifts. The money is good—I put extra aside for Meera's wedding fund this month—but I cannot remember the last time I sat down for a proper meal that was not eaten standing in the break room.

The new doctor on rotation asked why I work so much. I did not know how to explain that it is not really a choice. My brother's shop in Ahmedabad is struggling, my parents' medical bills keep growing, and Meera's wedding will not pay for itself. This is what daughters do. But then I missed the call with Appa, and I wonder if I am getting the balance wrong. He will not be around forever, and I keep choosing the extra shifts.

Nudge: "Do you actually think you're getting it wrong, or is that the exhaustion talking?"

Response: Both, maybe. Sometimes I think the money is the excuse. Easier to send the transfer than to sit with the silence on the phone when Appa forgets what he was saying mid-sentence. The shifts fill the time I would otherwise spend worrying.

Sunita auntie came over uninvited, which is very her. She brought that horrible orange mithai from the temple and sat in my kitchen for two hours talking about how her nephew just got married to "a good Gujarati girl." Then she looked at Meera's photo on the fridge and asked if there was "any news."

I told her Meera is focusing on her career right now, and Sunita auntie made that face. The one where her eyebrows go up and she says "career is good, but..." I just kept refilling her chai until she left. Did not argue, did not defend. Just smiled and nodded because that is what you do. And honestly, I was not even angry. This is just how aunties are. Meera will find someone when Meera finds someone, and no amount of auntie pressure will change that. I have made my peace with it even if they have not.

Nudge: "How did you get to that peace?"

Response: Watching Meera with her patients. She is good at what she does, really good. I did not have that when I was her age—I went into nursing because my mother did and her mother did. Meera chose it. That matters more than a wedding date.

Hospital was busy with the usual winter rush. Three admissions on my shift, two respiratory cases and one fall. Paperwork took longer than the actual care, which is frustrating but normal. The new electronic system is meant to make things faster but everyone is still learning it.

Brought leftover rotli from yesterday for lunch. Ate at my desk while filling in charts. Raj called to say he would be late picking up the groceries so I stopped at Tesco on the way home. Nothing interesting, just milk and bread and the biscuits Appa likes for when he visits next.

Back-to-back shifts this week. Monday bled into Tuesday and now it is whatever day it is. Slept for four hours between them and dreamed I was still at work, which felt cruel. Woke up confused about whether the patient in room 4B had actually been discharged or if I had just imagined it in the dream.

Made daal for dinner because it is the only thing that requires no thought. Watched something on telly but could not tell you what. Raj asked if I wanted to go for the walk we used to take around the park but I just wanted to sit. He sat with me instead. Neither of us said much. Sometimes that is enough.

Skipped the temple this morning. Told Raj I was too tired, which was true, but also I just did not feel like sitting through the puja with everyone asking about the family and when we are going to India next. Felt guilty about it all day.

Nudge: "What's the guilt actually about?"

Had a patient today who reminded me why I do this. Mrs. Okonkwo, eighty-three, recovering from a hip replacement. Her family is all in Nigeria and she kept asking when someone would come. I sat with her during my break, just talking about nothing—the food, the weather, whether the NHS would survive another winter. She held my hand and called me "daughter."

I know I cannot replace her actual children, but I can be there for the fifteen minutes between her medication rounds. Brought her a cup of chai from the nurses' station even though it is not exactly protocol. She said it reminded her of something her mother used to make. We are not so different, Mrs. Okonkwo and I. Both far from home, both making do.

Nudge: "What does 'making do' look like for you?"

Had the conversation with Meera about Canada again. She has a job offer in Toronto, a good one, at a hospital that specializes in what she wants to do. I should be proud, and I am proud, but I also spent the whole dinner feeling like I was swallowing something sharp every time she talked about visa applications and apartment hunting.

She said I was being quiet and I said I was just tired. Which was not a lie, but it was not the whole truth either. The truth is I keep thinking about how I moved to England and left my parents behind and now my daughter wants to do the same to me. It is not fair to think that way. She has every right to build her life. I did the same thing.

But Amma stayed. My grandmother stayed. All the women in our family stayed close, within calling distance, within sari-draping distance, within "come over for chai" distance. And I broke that chain when I came here, and now Meera is breaking it further, and I cannot decide if this is progress or loss. Raj says I should be happy she is ambitious. I am happy. I am also something else that does not have a word.

Nudge: "What's the something else?"

Meera and I cooked together this evening. She asked me to teach her the dhokla recipe, the one from my mother's side that we only make for special occasions. Said she wanted to learn before she goes. We stood in the kitchen for two hours, me showing her how to test the batter, her writing everything down in that little notebook she keeps.

She asked about Nani, questions she has never asked before. What songs she used to sing, what her hands looked like, whether she was strict or soft. I found myself telling stories I had not thought about in years. The way Nani would wake before sunrise to pray. The way she saved the best pieces of mango for the grandchildren. Meera listened like she was memorising it all, and maybe she was. Maybe this is how it continues—not by staying in the same place, but by carrying the recipes and the stories wherever you go.

Nudge: "Do you believe that—that carrying the stories is enough?"

Response: Ask me again in a year when she is actually gone.

Sent the dhokla recipe to my mother. She called immediately, laughing, saying I had written down the measurements when she never measures anything. Told her Meera made her promise to keep it exactly as written. Three generations passing the same batter between their hands.

Nudge: "How did it feel, sending it?"

Response: Like handing over something I did not know I was holding. Heavy and light at the same time.

Long shift today—twelve hours on the pediatric oncology floor. Lost a patient, a seven-year-old boy named Diego. His mother was inconsolable, and I stayed an extra hour just sitting with her, holding her hand because that's what my own mother would have done. That's what we do in my family. You don't leave people alone with grief.

I came home and lit a candle at my altar. Said a prayer for Diego, then another for his family. It felt right to do something, even if it's small. The other nurses on my floor, the American ones, they cope by going out for drinks or hitting the gym. That's fine for them. But I need the quiet. I need the ritual. It centers me.

Called Mami afterward. She didn't say much, just listened while I cried a little. Then she reminded me that this work is holy, that caring for the sick is what God asks of us. I don't know if I believe exactly what she believes anymore, but I believe in that feeling—that this job matters, that being present for people in their worst moments is something sacred.

I'm exhausted but I don't regret the extra hour. I'd do it again tomorrow.

Had coffee with my cousin Marisol after work. We talked about maybe getting an apartment together next year—it would cut costs and she's family, so I trust her. It's nice to have someone nearby who understands why I do things the way I do.

Nudge: "What would it mean for you to have Marisol closer in your daily life?"

Posadas season started this week. The hospital let me organize a small celebration for the pediatric patients who were well enough to participate. We made paper luminarias, sang "Pidiendo Posada," and I brought in tamales that my tía made. Some of the kids had never seen anything like it—they kept asking questions about why we go door to door, why we carry the figures of Mary and Joseph.

Explaining it to them made me realize how much I've been holding onto these things without really thinking about why. It's not just about religion, not really. It's about belonging to something bigger than myself. Something that connects me to my grandmother, my great-grandmother, and all the way back. When I sing those songs, I can hear my abuela's voice mixing with mine, even though she's thousands of miles away.

One of the other nurses, Jessica, said she thought it was "so cool" that I have all these customs. She said her family doesn't really do anything special for the holidays anymore. I felt sad for her. Not in a judgy way, just—I can't imagine not having that thread connecting me to where I come from. It would feel like floating.

I'm already planning what to bring for Nochebuena. The head nurse said I could have Christmas Eve off if I work Christmas Day, which is perfect because Christmas Eve is when my family has always gathered anyway.

My supervisor offered me a position on the travel nursing team. Better pay, opportunity to see different parts of the country. I told her I'd think about it but I already know my answer.

The money would help—I could send more home, save faster for the condo. But travel nursing means no consistent schedule, no knowing where I'll be month to month. My cousin is counting on me for the apartment plan. And I can't just leave Miami when my whole support system is here. Abuela wasn't thrilled when I left Guatemala; leaving again feels like I'd be proving something wrong.

Nudge: "It sounds like there might be more pulling at you here than just the practical decision. What feels hardest to say out loud?"

Response: The hardest thing to say is that part of me is curious. Part of me wonders what it would be like to just go somewhere new, not have to be the one everyone depends on for a minute. But that's selfish. My family gave up a lot so I could be here. I'm supposed to be building something stable, not chasing adventures. That's not who I am.

Spent Nochebuena with my cousins and their families. Marisol's mother made the tamales this year, and we all said they weren't as good as my tía's back home, but we ate them anyway and laughed about it. That's the thing about being far from home—you recreate what you can, and the imperfections become part of the new story.

Midnight mass was crowded. I sat between Marisol and her little brother, and when we sang "Noche de Paz," I closed my eyes and could almost pretend I was back in the cathedral in Guatemala City, the one with the blue walls and the golden altar. The incense smelled the same. Some things carry across distance.

Now it's New Year's Eve and I'm home early from a short shift. The city is loud with fireworks and parties, but I'm content to stay in. I cleaned my apartment, changed the water for my santos, and made a list of things I want to accomplish next year: get the condo, help Marisol settle in, call home more often, maybe learn to make mole the way my grandmother does.

It's strange to think about the year ending. I feel like I'm exactly where I should be—maybe not where I dreamed as a teenager, but where I'm meant to be. There's comfort in that. Knowing that I'm building something solid, something that will last.

Started the new year by setting up automatic transfers to my savings account. It's not much, but seeing that number grow a little each month makes me feel like I'm actually getting somewhere. Small steps.

Nudge: "Where is 'somewhere' for you? What does getting there look like?"

Had an argument with my coworker Rachel today. She made a comment about how I'm "always on the phone with my mom" and said it in a way that felt like she was making fun of me. I told her that in my family, we actually talk to each other, and she got quiet after that. Maybe I was too harsh but I don't regret it.

I know Americans have different relationships with their families. I know not everyone has a mother they can call whenever they need to hear a familiar voice. But the way she said it—like I'm some kind of child who can't function without parental approval—that stung. I'm not asking for permission when I call Mami. I'm asking for connection. There's a difference.

And yes, maybe I do ask her opinion on things more than Rachel asks her mother. So what? My mother has lived through things Rachel can't imagine. She raised four children while my father worked two jobs. She kept our family together when half the neighborhood was falling apart. Her opinion matters to me because she's earned it.

I'm not going to change who I am to make Rachel comfortable. If she thinks I'm too close to my family, that's her problem. I'd rather be "too close" than whatever she has going on where she barely speaks to her parents once a month.

Nudge: "Underneath the frustration, what did Rachel's comment touch in you?"

Response: It touched the part of me that sometimes wonders if I'm holding on too tight. If maybe the way I was raised doesn't fit perfectly here. I don't want to question how my family does things—we've been this way for generations and it's gotten us through everything. But sometimes I feel caught between two worlds, and comments like Rachel's just remind me that I don't fully belong in either one.

The condo I was looking at fell through. Someone else made a cash offer and the seller took it. Marisol said we could just find another one, but she doesn't get it. That was the building where our other cousins live. That was the whole point.

Everyone keeps telling me it's fine, that there will be other places, but I had planned around this. I had a whole picture in my head: Marisol and me in our place, walking distance from Lucia and her kids, Sunday dinners that don't require an hour of driving. Now I have to start over and I don't even know what I'm looking for anymore.

I talked to Abuela today. Really talked—not just the usual check-in where we say we're fine and miss each other. I told her about the condo, about feeling stuck, about Rachel and the travel nursing offer and all of it. She listened for a long time without interrupting, which is unusual for her.

Then she told me a story I'd never heard. About when she was young, maybe a little younger than me, and she moved from her village to Guatemala City for work. She said she cried every night for months. She said the city people made fun of how she talked, how she dressed, the food she cooked. She said she thought about going home so many times she lost count.

But she stayed. She stayed because she could see something on the other side of the hard part—a life she could build, children she could raise in a place with more opportunities. She said the crying stopped eventually. Not because it got easier, but because she grew into the person who could handle it.

"Mija," she said, "you're already that person. You just don't see it yet."

I cried after we hung up. Good tears, though. The kind that clean something out. I don't know if I'll find a condo in that building or if Marisol and I will end up somewhere else entirely. I don't know if I'll ever stop feeling caught between here and home. But I know that whatever I build, it will have roots. It will have her in it, and Mami, and everyone who came before.

That's enough. That has to be enough.

Nudge: "What would it look like if it were simply 'enough'—without the 'has to be'?"

Response: I think it would look like trusting that I'm not going to lose anything by being here. That keeping our ways alive doesn't require living in the same building as my cousins or calling Mami every single day. That the roots can stretch without breaking. I'm not there yet. But maybe I'm closer than I was this morning.

Celine asked me again today if I'd reconsidered the flatshare. She found a third person, a ceramicist she knows from art school, and said it would bring my rent down by almost three hundred euros a month. I told her I'd think about it, which is what I always say. The truth is I already ran the numbers. Even with the savings, the lease is month-to-month, the building doesn't have proper insurance, and I have no idea if these two people pay on time.

I know she thinks I'm making excuses. She said something like, "You could use that money for supplies," which is true, but supplies don't matter if I can't pay rent in December because someone flaked. I stayed quiet after that and she changed the subject. I keep having this feeling like I'm making the boring choice and everyone can see it, but I also know I'd lie awake every night in that flat wondering when the arrangement would fall apart.

Nudge: "What would that place need to have for you to say yes?"

Response: A proper lease. All three names on it, with a clause about what happens if someone leaves. Basically the same thing I have now but cheaper. Which I know is the whole reason they don't want to do it that way. So there's my answer, I guess.

Frame shop was quiet today. Restrung two canvases, swept up, closed early because Monsieur Girard had a dentist appointment. Walked home along the Rhone and picked up bread on the way. Nothing to report.

Got the confirmation email for the autumn group show at Galerie Moreau. Three pieces, one wall, six weeks. I actually teared up a little, which is embarrassing to write. But I've been working toward this since January and it's the first time someone picked my work without me having to explain it first.

Spent the morning at the bank because the online transfer for the savings account wasn't going through. Turns out there's a new verification step they added and I needed to update my phone number in person. So that was an hour and a half of my day gone, sitting in a plastic chair watching a number on a screen count up. But I got it sorted. The auto-transfer is set for the 2nd of every month again and the buffer is back where it should be.

The part that's sitting with me is what the woman at the counter said. She asked what the savings were for and I said it was just a general reserve. She sort of smiled and said, "Most people your age don't bother." She meant it as a compliment, I think. But it made me feel strange, like I'm doing something unusual by not wanting to end up stuck. Like it's weird to have a plan for what happens if the freelance work dries up or if Girard closes the shop.

I don't think it's weird. My parents went through a period when I was fifteen where money was genuinely scary, and I remember what that did to my mother's face for about two years. I just don't want to be in that position. But hearing someone point out that most people my age don't do this — I don't know. It made the whole thing feel heavier than it needs to be.

Nudge: "What was it about your mother's face specifically?"

Response: I don't know, it's not one thing. She just looked tired all the time in a way that went deeper than tired. Like she was doing calculations in her head constantly. Anyway, the point is the transfer is fixed now. I'll check it next month to make sure it actually went through automatically.

Back from the bank thing this morning, then spent the afternoon finishing the second piece for the Moreau show. Ink work on the hands came out wrong twice and I had to start the panel over. Eyes are burning. Going to eat leftovers and sleep.

Had a really good afternoon at the shop. A woman came in to get an old watercolour framed — her grandmother's, she said. We spent maybe twenty minutes going through mat options and she kept asking my opinion like it actually mattered. I ended up suggesting a linen mount with a thin oak frame, which is what I would have done for my own work. She seemed genuinely happy. Girard nodded at me from the back, which from him is basically a standing ovation.

I think the reason the frame shop works for me, beyond the money, is that it's predictable. I know what Tuesdays look like. I know what Girard expects. There's a rhythm to it that makes the freelance days feel less chaotic by comparison. Some of my friends think I should quit and go full-time on illustration, and maybe they're right eventually, but right now having those three shifts is like having a floor under me. Everything else can wobble as long as the floor holds.

Nudge: "Is the floor something you want to keep, or something you want to stop needing?"

Had dinner with Celine and Hugo last night. Hugo just quit his job at the design agency to freelance full-time. No savings, no backup plan, just "I'll figure it out." Everyone at the table was congratulating him like he'd done something brave. Celine looked at me at one point and said, "See? It's possible." As if I don't know it's possible. That's not the question.

The question is what happens when it doesn't work out and there's nothing underneath you. But I didn't say that, because I'd sound like someone's aunt. I just smiled and said good for him. I meant it, mostly. But the whole walk home I was annoyed, not at Hugo specifically, just at this idea that keeping something steady is the same as not trying. I am trying. I'm showing at Moreau in October. I just also happen to have rent covered through March regardless of whether anyone buys anything. That's not fear, that's just not being stupid.

Wednesday. Frame shop in the morning, then home to work on the third Moreau piece. This one's coming together faster than the other two — the composition sorted itself out in my sketchbook last week and I've mostly been executing. Used the good ink I've been rationing since April. Felt right to use it now rather than save it for something hypothetical.

After dinner I updated my invoice spreadsheet. Two freelance payments came in this week — the book cover for that small press in Marseille, and the editorial spot for the food magazine. Both on time, which doesn't always happen. Logged them, moved the savings portion over, updated the running total. It's not a lot but it's growing. At this rate I'll hit my year-end target by November.

I also renewed my phone contract today instead of switching to the cheaper provider Celine recommended. Her provider has better rates but they lock you into a twelve-month plan with an exit fee, and I've heard mixed things about their coverage outside the city. Mine costs eight euros more per month but I've had it for four years and it just works. Sometimes I think the small extra cost is worth not having to think about it.

Nudge: "You're paying for certainty a lot — the lease, the phone, the frame shop. Is that ever too much?"

Response: Maybe. I add it up sometimes and think, if I dropped all the safe choices I'd have an extra four or five hundred euros a month. That's materials, that's a short course, that's two months closer to not needing the frame shop at all. But then I imagine actually doing it and my chest gets tight. It's like — I know the maths says take the risk, but my body won't let me. I don't know if that's wisdom or just a thing I haven't worked through yet.

Girard mentioned today he might cut hours in September because business slows down after the tourists leave. Just a possibility, he said. But I've already started thinking about what I'd do if he cuts my shifts to two, or one, or none.

Signed Arjun up for the Math Olympiad prep course at Kumon even though his regular tuition teacher said he wasn't ready. The prep costs $480 a month on top of his existing classes and I didn't tell Ramesh yet. Not because he'd say no -- he never says no to anything for the kids -- but because I don't want to explain why I pushed for it. The tuition teacher was careful about it, said Arjun is solid but not exceptional, that maybe we should wait a year. And I heard myself saying he'll rise to the challenge, that's how we've always done it, throw him in and watch him swim.

But the car ride home was quiet. Arjun was in the backseat reading some manga and he didn't ask a single question about what just happened. He doesn't know I enrolled him. I was sitting at the traffic light near Bukit Timah and I thought, am I doing this for him or am I doing this because Kavitha aunty's son got into the Olympiad last year and I spent three months hearing about it at every family gathering. Priya brought it up at the temple too. Her Rohan this, her Rohan that.

I used to be the one people talked about. CA at twenty-three. First in the family to work at a Big Four. Now the only scoreboard I have is my children's report cards and I read them like they're my own performance reviews. Knowing something is off and stopping it are two very different things.

Nudge: "What happens when Arjun finds out?"

Response: He'll do what he always does. Nod, pick up the workbook, sit at the dining table after dinner. He's obedient like that. Sometimes I wish he'd push back so I'd have a reason to question whether I'm pushing too hard. But he just absorbs it, and I keep adding.

Amma made pongal rice this morning without being asked. Just woke up early, did the tempering with the curry leaves and cashews the way she's done it for sixty years, and had it ready by the time the kids came down. Arjun ate three servings. Even Divya, who's been on some ridiculous diet she read about on TikTok, had a full plate. And for a moment the kitchen smelled exactly like my grandmother's house in Mylapore, that particular combination of ghee and mustard seeds and the brass vessels heating up.

I sat there and I was furious. Not at Amma. At myself. Because I can't make it taste the same. I've watched her a hundred times, written the recipe down twice, and mine is always missing something. She says it's the hand -- amma kai paakkum -- that the food knows who is cooking it. Which is the most unscientific thing anyone has ever said to me and also the truest thing I've heard in years.

She's seventy-eight. Her knees are bad and she can't stand for long anymore. She did this today because Arjun mentioned he missed her pongal rice last week and she heard him. She heard him and she got up at five in the morning for it. I need to learn it properly this time. Not from a recipe card. From standing next to her and doing it until my hands remember.

Spent the whole morning reorganizing the prayer room shelf because Amma said the Lakshmi lamp was in the wrong position. Then drove Divya to violin, Arjun to Kumon, picked up Sanjay's cricket whites from the tailor. Calendar is a Tetris board this week.

Nudge: "Was moving the lamp just another task or something else?"

Response: It needed to be done. Amma asked, I did it. Not everything has to mean something.

Ramesh asked why the credit card had a new recurring charge and I told him about Kumon. He just said okay, didn't even look up from his laptop. I don't know why I was bracing for a fight that was never going to happen. Maybe I wanted him to have an opinion about it.

Divya asked if she could skip the temple youth group meeting this Saturday for a friend's birthday party. I said no before I even thought about it. She went to her room and closed the door quietly, which was worse than slamming it.

Nudge: "Why was the quiet door worse?"

Response: Because slamming means she's angry and anger I can handle. Quiet means she's given up trying to negotiate. It means she already knows the answer before she asks and she's just going through the motions. I recognize it because I used to do the same thing with my own mother. Ask, get told no, retreat. I thought I'd be different but I'm running the same script.

Pongal committee meeting at the community centre. Finalized the vendor list and stage layout. Kumaresh uncle questioned my budget for the second year in a row and I had the spreadsheet ready this time.

Arjun's first Olympiad practice test came back. Forty-second percentile. His regular tuition teacher called to check in and I could hear the I-told-you-so in her voice even though she was perfectly polite about it.

Nudge: "What did you feel when you saw the score?"

Response: It's one test. Baseline. That's the whole point of a baseline -- you start somewhere.

Long week. Sanjay had a cricket tournament in Jurong, two matches on Saturday and one Sunday morning. I was there for all of them, sitting in the pavilion with the other parents, making small talk about school rankings and enrichment classes. The usual tournament of its own, except we're the ones competing and the kids are just the scorecards.

Amma had a fall in the bathroom on Sunday while we were out. Nothing broken, but she bruised her hip and now she's moving even slower. Ramesh wants to talk about getting a helper but Amma won't hear of it. Says a stranger in the kitchen will disrupt the house. She means disrupt her routines -- the morning puja, the way she arranges the spice boxes, the specific order she hangs clothes on the bamboo poles. I understand it. I just don't know how much longer we can pretend she doesn't need help.

Nudge: "Where do you land on the helper question?"

Response: Stuck. I agree with Ramesh practically but I can't be the one who tells Amma her way of running a house isn't sustainable anymore. She built this life, this order. Taking it apart feels like telling her she's done. And she'll look at me like I've betrayed something. I'm not ready for that look.

Divya played her violin recital piece for us after dinner and it was genuinely beautiful. Mendelssohn. She's been grinding at it for months and tonight she played it clean, start to finish, no stops. Sanjay actually put his phone down to listen. Amma was in her chair nodding along. Ramesh recorded the whole thing on his phone.

And when she finished I clapped and told her it was wonderful and she smiled -- real smile, not the one she uses to get through family photos -- and I felt my chest crack open a little. This girl who closes doors quietly and rolls her eyes at temple and fights me on every single thing she can. She stood there with the violin under her chin and she was radiant. I wanted to tell her that but I couldn't figure out how to say it without making it about her exam grade or her college application. So I just said "beautiful, kanna" and hoped it was enough.

Arjun's Olympiad score went up to sixty-first percentile on the latest test. He showed me himself this time, brought the paper to the kitchen counter while I was chopping onions. I could tell he was pleased but trying not to make a big thing of it. I said good, keep going, and he nodded and went back to his room. Simple exchange but it sat with me.

I keep thinking about what my father used to say when I brought home my marks -- he'd scan the paper, circle whatever I got wrong, and say "next time, full marks." No praise for what I'd already done. Just the gap. I told myself I wouldn't do that to my kids but I catch myself doing a version of it. Keep going. As if where he is right now doesn't count. As if the sixty-one is just a waypoint to wherever I've decided he should end up. He looked happy at the counter. I should have said that's really good, Arjun. I should have let the moment be what it was.

Nudge: "Could you still tell him?"

My mother-in-law came by today and made some comment about the stack of translation documents on the kitchen table. Something about how I should focus on the kids instead of "outside projects." I smiled and said I'd clear them after lunch. But it sat with me the rest of the afternoon.

She doesn't understand what those documents are. Each one is someone's asylum application or work permit or medical form that they can't read because nobody thought to print it in a language they actually speak. I can do that. I can sit at my kitchen table after the kids are asleep and make that one thing less terrifying for someone. And my kids are fed, they're healthy, they're fine. I don't need to justify this to anyone, but I keep feeling like I have to.

Nudge: "You said you don't need to justify it. So why does it still sit with you?"

Response: Because she's not wrong that there are only so many hours. I chose to stay home for the kids. But the work I left behind didn't stop needing to be done. I just can't shake the feeling that I'm supposed to pick one and be at peace with it, and I haven't figured out how to do that yet.

Residents association meeting tonight about the new recycling sorting rules the district is rolling out. I volunteered to run the information session for our building because nobody else would and the flyer they sent was confusing — mixing up the plastic codes and getting the compost pickup schedule wrong. Spent two hours after the kids' bedtime redoing the flyer in Korean and English since we have several non-Korean families in the complex.

Hyun-woo got home late again and asked why I was still up. I showed him the flyer and he said "can't someone else do this?" He wasn't being mean. He just doesn't get why I'd spend a free evening on something that could theoretically be someone else's problem.

Nudge: "What would happen if you'd just left the flyer as it was?"

Response: The wrong information would go out and our building would get fined again, and the two Filipina families on the fourth floor who can barely read the Korean notices would have no idea what changed. I keep thinking about Mrs. Reyes carrying her bags to the wrong bin last month looking confused and embarrassed. Nobody helped her. I can't see that and not do something. It physically bothers me to walk past a problem I could fix.

Took the kids to the park near the river this morning. Eun-bi wanted to feed the ducks and Jun-ho wanted to throw rocks, so we compromised — ducks first, then rocks at a different spot. Standard negotiation. It was actually nice until I noticed a group of volunteers doing a river cleanup downstream. They had those grabber tools and black trash bags and matching vests from some environmental group I didn't recognize.

I stood there watching them for probably five minutes while the kids played. Part of me wanted to walk over and ask how to join. I used to do cleanups like that every month before Jun-ho was born. Our old NGO partnered with a river conservation group in Mapo-gu and I'd spend whole Saturdays hauling plastic out of the Cheonggyecheon tributaries. Felt like I was doing something tangible, something I could point at and say today this stretch of water is cleaner because I was here.

Now I watch from the playground. I'm not complaining about my life — the kids are wonderful and I chose this. But there's this gap between what I used to be able to do and what my days look like now. I don't know what to do with that gap except notice it and move on. Jun-ho threw a rock into the water and it made a perfect splash and he was so proud. I clapped and said "good one" and we went home for lunch.

Can't sleep. Kept thinking about the river cleanup people. Found their group online — they meet every Saturday, which is the one morning Hyun-woo is reliably home with the kids. I could go. But then I'd be giving up the only morning I have for grocery shopping, meal prep, and the laundry pile that's been staring at me all week.

My friend Ji-yeon texted asking if I wanted to join her yoga class on Saturday mornings. Everyone keeps offering me ways to spend my time that are about me. Yoga, self-care, "you need a break." But I don't want a break. I want to be useful beyond these walls. That probably sounds ungrateful. I have a warm apartment and two healthy children and a husband who comes home every night. But I keep reading the news about the factory fire in Gimpo where those migrant workers were locked in because the exits were blocked and I can't just — I can't just do yoga about it.

Went to the refugee resettlement office for my translation shift. They had a backlog of about forty documents — medical intake forms, mostly. One was for a family from Myanmar with three kids under ten. The mother had written additional notes in the margins in Burmese that I couldn't read, so I flagged them for the Burmese translator. Just small handwritten lines. Probably questions she was too afraid to ask out loud. That kind of thing breaks something in me every time.

Came home and Eun-bi had drawn a picture of our family at school. She drew me holding a big stack of papers. Hyun-woo laughed and said "even she knows." I laughed too but it landed weird. Like even my six-year-old sees me as the woman with the papers instead of — I don't know. I don't know what I expected her to draw. I am the woman with the papers. I just wish that didn't feel like it needed defending.

Nudge: "What did you want her to draw instead?"

Both kids had fevers last night so I was up every two hours with wet towels and children's Tylenol. Jun-ho finally slept at 4am. I skipped the recycling committee meeting and I feel guilty about it even though I know that's absurd.

Nudge: "Why does skipping it feel like a failure?"

Response: Because I told Mrs. Park I'd present the updated sorting schedule and now she has to scramble. I know she'll manage. I know my kids come first. But letting someone down — even for a completely valid reason — just eats at me. It always has.

Kids are better. Jun-ho's fever broke around noon and Eun-bi was already bouncing off the walls by breakfast. Hyun-woo took the afternoon off which almost never happens, so I had about three hours to myself. I should have napped. Instead I sat on the balcony with my laptop and read through the news.

There's a piece about a proposed bill that would make it harder for undocumented workers to access emergency medical care. I read it twice. Then I started drafting a letter to our local representative, citing cases I'd seen at the resettlement office — anonymized, of course. I wrote about the Myanmar family's intake forms and the gap between what the system promises and what reaches people. It took me two hours and I don't even know if anyone will read it.

Hyun-woo came in and saw me typing and said nothing. He just made me tea and left it on the desk. I think he's given up trying to understand why I spend my rare free hours this way. He's not angry. He's just resigned. And I can feel the distance in that resignation. Like he's accepted that this is who I am but he'd prefer a version of me who could sit on the balcony and just look at the sky. Some days I'd prefer that version too. But I open the news and there's another factory fire or another deportation story or another kid who fell through the cracks and I can't look at the sky. The sky is not the problem.

Sent the letter. Also emailed the river cleanup group about joining their Saturday rotation starting August. Hyun-woo agreed to handle Saturday mornings. Feels like a small step but at least it's forward.

First Saturday cleanup. Hauled three bags of plastic from a 200-meter stretch near Banpo Bridge. Got home muddy and sunburned. Eun-bi asked if I was "saving the river" and I said I was trying.

Nudge: "How did it feel being back out there?"

Spent the morning reviewing proposals from two potential investors. One offers more capital but wants a board seat and quarterly reviews. The other is smaller but completely hands-off. Everyone keeps telling me the bigger check is the obvious choice. But I keep thinking about three years ago when I walked away from a salary that would make most people's eyes water, all because I couldn't stand someone else dictating my calendar.

Maybe I'm being naive. Maybe smart founders take the bigger check and learn to play the game. But every time I imagine sitting in those quarterly reviews, justifying my decisions to people who've never built anything themselves, something in my chest tightens.

Nudge: "What does that tightening in your chest usually signal for you?"

Response: It's the same feeling I had in my last corporate job. Before I quit. Like I'm slowly being boxed in. When I was younger I thought I'd outgrow it, learn to compromise. But if anything it's gotten stronger. I think that feeling is my compass now—when it shows up, I know I'm about to betray something important about how I want to live.
Took the smaller investment. Signed papers yesterday. Celebrated with Layla at that new Persian restaurant near the Marina. Felt right.

My mother called from Tehran today. She wanted to know when I'm getting married, when I'm having children, when I'm going to "settle down and stop playing." I'm almost forty. I've heard these questions my whole adult life. Usually I brush them off. Today it got under my skin more than usual.

I think it's because part of me wonders if she has a point. Not about marriage specifically—I'll figure that out when I figure it out. But about what I'm building toward. I work hard, yes, but I also make sure to enjoy the fruits of that work. Weekends in Oman. Good wine. A nice apartment. She sees all of that as frivolous, as avoiding responsibility. In her world, you sacrifice everything for family, for the future, for some distant reward.

But I've watched people sacrifice their entire lives for that distant reward and then die before they get there. Or they get there and realize they forgot how to enjoy anything. I don't want that. I refuse that. And yet—today, after we hung up, I sat on my balcony for an hour just staring at the water, wondering if my version of a good life will leave me alone at sixty with nothing but nice memories.

Nudge: "It sounds like you're weighing two different visions of what a meaningful life looks like. Which one feels more true to who you are right now?"

Response: The one where I'm present. Where I'm actually living my days instead of deferring them. I've tried the other way—grinding through miserable weeks for some hypothetical payoff. It made me sick, literally. Migraines, insomnia. My body rejected it. So I know what's true for me. I just wish knowing made it easier to hear my mother's disappointment.

Layla and I got into it last night. She thinks I work too much. I think that's rich coming from someone who doesn't understand what it takes to run your own thing. When you're employed, you clock out and your problems stay at the office. When it's YOUR company, it follows you everywhere.

But then—I don't know. She made this comment about how I used to be more fun. More spontaneous. And it stung because she's not wrong. The last few months I've been grinding harder than usual, trying to prove to myself that taking the smaller check was the right call. Trying to show I can grow this thing without giving up control.

I hate that I'm becoming the person I swore I wouldn't become. The one who's always "busy."

Nudge: "You mentioned you 'hate becoming the person you swore you wouldn't become.' What would that person look like if you fully became them?"

Response: I don't want to think about that right now. I just needed to get this out. I know what I need to do—I need to take her somewhere nice for the holidays and actually be present. That's the plan.

We went to Muscat for three days. Left my laptop at home—Layla's one condition. First day I was restless, kept reaching for my phone. By the third day I had forgotten what I was even anxious about.

We hiked to this natural pool in the mountains. Cold water, complete silence except for birds. I floated on my back and watched clouds move and thought: this is why I built my own thing. Not for the money, not for the status. For moments like this, whenever I want them. The ability to disappear for three days without asking permission.

I need to protect this. The business should serve the life, not the other way around.

Back from holiday. January pipeline already overwhelming. Three new client requests, two urgent. I said yes to all of them because I'm still in growth mode. Already regretting it.

Nudge: "When you say you're already regretting it, what specifically is showing up for you?"

Response: The familiar dread. Knowing the next two months will be meetings and deadlines and no room to breathe. Knowing I just had a beautiful reset and I'm already overcommitting. It's a pattern. I recognize it. I just don't know how to break it when saying no feels like leaving money on the table.

My old colleague Faisal reached out. He's now a VP at a big consulting firm, wanted to know if I'd consider coming back to corporate. "Senior role, name your salary, you'd have a team of twenty."

I said no, obviously. But then he made this comment—"You're still doing the solo thing? I thought that was a phase." And I felt this flash of anger. Like I needed to justify myself. Started listing my clients, my revenue, my growth numbers. Trying to prove I wasn't some hobbyist.

Later I wondered why I cared so much. Why his opinion mattered. I left that world for a reason. I don't owe anyone an explanation.

Nudge: "What do you think Faisal's comment touched on that made you want to prove yourself?"

Response: Maybe the fear that I'm fooling myself. That everyone else sees something I don't—that I gave up a real career to play entrepreneur, and one day it'll all fall apart and I'll have nothing to show for it. I know that's not rational. My business is healthy. But there's this voice that sounds a lot like my father, saying I'm not serious enough. That real men build empires, not lifestyles. I thought I'd silenced that voice. Apparently not.

Funny thing happened today. One of my clients—a resort chain—asked if I'd relocate to Bahrain for six months to oversee an opening. Great money, interesting project. My immediate reaction was no. Not because of the work, but because I imagined six months without my Thursday dinners at that Lebanese place, without my morning runs along the creek, without the routines that make my life here feel like mine.

Then I caught myself and thought—wait, am I becoming rigid? I built this life precisely so I could be flexible. So I could say yes to adventures. But now I'm protecting routines like they're sacred.

Or maybe routines are sacred. Maybe I've earned the right to build a life I don't want to escape from. Maybe flexibility isn't about saying yes to every opportunity but about having the freedom to say no.

I don't know. I told them I'd think about it over the weekend.

Nudge: "You mentioned earning 'the right to build a life you don't want to escape from.' What did it cost you to earn that right?"

Turned down the Bahrain project. Explained that I could consult remotely but wouldn't relocate. They accepted, which surprised me. I expected them to walk away. Maybe I've been underestimating my leverage. Maybe I've been saying yes to things out of scarcity when I could afford to negotiate.

Layla was relieved. She said, "You're finally acting like someone who knows what he wants." I think she's right. For years I was running from things—from my old career, from expectations, from boredom. Now I'm starting to run toward something specific. A life that actually fits. It's a subtle shift but it feels significant.

We're planning another trip for March. Somewhere new. The reward for good work isn't more work—it's more life.

Started prepping canvases for the November show this morning. Same dimensions I always use for the parish hall walls -- 80 by 120, because the hooks are fixed in place and nobody is going to drill new ones into century-old plaster. There's something steadying about knowing exactly where each piece will hang before I've even mixed a color.

Spent the afternoon sketching from the bridge near Engis. The light this time of year turns the river into something between pewter and bronze, and I keep trying to pin it down the way Grand-mere did in her later work. I'm not sure I've gotten it right yet. Maybe I never will, but the attempt itself feels like the point. Brought the kids after school and let them toss stones while I worked. Ordinary afternoon.

Nudge: "What does getting it 'right' mean to you here?"

Response: Probably less about the painting and more about the river itself.

Parish hall booking confirmed for November 15-16. Same weekend as last year. Father Renaud said they're replacing the radiators in the vestibule, but the main hall should be unaffected. Spoke with Maman about the catering -- she'll do the tarte al djote again and Tante Sylvie offered to bring the boulets. This is the seventh year running we've kept to the same menu.

The framer in Jupille finished mounting three pieces. I went to collect them and he'd used the same molding profile Grand-mere specified in her notebook -- simple oak, no gilding. I didn't ask him to match it; he just remembered from previous years. Small thing, but it matters. There's a continuity in those details that makes the whole event feel less like something I organize and more like something that carries itself forward.

Spent the evening sorting through old catalogues from Maman's early exhibitions. Found a small pencil sketch she'd tucked inside the 1988 programme -- a quick study of the church door. I might frame it and include it in my show as a kind of anchor piece. Not sure yet. Need to ask her first.

Nudge: "Why does asking her first feel necessary?"

A gallerist from Brussels came by the studio yesterday. She'd seen some photos online and wanted to know if I'd be interested in showing at her space in Ixelles. Bigger audience, better lighting, actual opening night press. She said the parish hall thing was "charming" but suggested I was limiting my reach.

I know she meant it as a compliment, or at least as professional advice. But I didn't ask for professional advice. The November show isn't about reach. It's about the fact that people from the neighborhood come back each year and some of them remember Grand-mere's work on those same walls. That means something that a Brussels gallery opening with wine and small talk cannot replicate. I told her I'd think about it, but I already know the answer.

My daughter asked why we always have to go to Papy and Mamy's on Sundays. She wants to do football practice with her friends instead. I didn't have a good answer -- just that we go because we've always gone. Hearing it out loud, it sounded hollow even to me.

Still turning over what Elise said this morning. She's nine. She doesn't understand yet why we keep these rhythms, and I shouldn't expect her to. But the question caught me off guard because I've never had to justify it before. To anyone. Not even to myself.

My partner mentioned, gently, that maybe the Sunday lunches could shift to every other week. That the kids are getting older and their schedules are filling up. He wasn't attacking anything -- he was being practical. But it felt like the start of something eroding. If we skip one week, then two, then the meal becomes a monthly obligation that people rearrange their calendars around rather than something that simply is.

I know that's not rational. Things have to bend sometimes. I just don't want to be the one who let them break. Three generations of Sunday lunches in that kitchen. My grandfather built the long table specifically because he wanted everyone to sit together. It wasn't incidental. It was a decision, and decisions like that deserve to be honored even when they stop being convenient.

Nudge: "What would 'letting them break' actually look like?"

Response: Empty chairs. The table feeling too big. Maman cooking for two instead of twelve.

Went to the studio early. Needed to just paint without thinking about schedules or compromises. Worked on the largest canvas for the show -- the one based on the view from the mill bridge. It's the same view Grand-mere painted in 1971, and Maman in 1994, and now me. Three versions of the same bend in the river, each a little different because the trees change and the banks shift, but the bones of it stay.

A friend who paints digitally told me last week that I should try working on a tablet. Faster, cheaper, infinite undo. I told him I like the smell of linseed oil and the resistance of the canvas. He laughed like I was joking. I wasn't. The materials matter. The process matters. You don't inherit a craft by switching to whatever's newest. You inherit it by keeping your hands in the same medium.

Nudge: "Is it the craft you're keeping, or something else?"

Response: Both. The craft and the line of people who practiced it before me.

We went to Papy and Mamy's yesterday. Elise came without complaint but spent most of the meal drawing on a napkin instead of talking. I watched her and wondered if I'm passing something down or just dragging her through motions that only mean something to me.

Took the finished canvases to the parish hall for a test hang. The light from the west window hit the river painting exactly the way it hits Grand-mere's piece in the catalogue photo from 1978. Same angle, same hour, same wall. Some things just line up when you let them.

Exhibition prep is wearing me thin. Maman called to say she might not do the tarte this year -- her hands are bothering her and she doesn't want to push it. I told her it's fine, but after I hung up I just sat in the studio staring at nothing for a while.

Nudge: "What were you sitting with in that silence?"

Noor brought up the London program again at dinner. I could feel my chest tighten but I just said we'd talk later and cleared the plates. Haven't been able to sleep since.

Nudge: "What part of her bringing it up hit you the hardest?"

Response: She'd already looked up flat prices. Decided without me.

Spent the morning making maamoul with Hana and Tariq. They came over early, flour everywhere, the kitchen smelled like the house used to when Samira was alive. Hana shaped hers exactly the way her mother did, pressing the mold with both palms. I watched her and something settled inside me, like proof that what I've kept going all these years still matters to them too.

Tariq asked if we could do this every Friday, not just during holidays. I said of course. Small thing, but it sat well with me the rest of the afternoon.

Nudge: "Why did Tariq's request matter so much?"

My brother Walid called and said I should let Noor go, that I'm being selfish. Easy for him to say from Dubai with his kids in boarding school. Nobody in this family understands that keeping people close isn't the same as holding them back.

Came back to this tonight because Walid's call kept eating at me. After I hung up I sat in the garden for a long time. The jasmine Samira planted is still growing along the back wall, fifteen years now. I thought about how she would have handled this. She was always braver about letting the children try things. I think she would have been scared too but she wouldn't have let the fear win.

I don't know if that changes what I'll say to Noor. But I realized the anger I felt toward Walid was really just the sound of my own doubt getting louder.

Nudge: "You said Samira wouldn't have let fear win. What's the fear, exactly?"

Response: That if she leaves, the rest follow. House falls apart.

Noor cooked mansaf tonight. The whole thing, from scratch, without asking for help. She set the platter on the table the way I always do, rice shaped just right. Felt like she was showing me something without saying it.

Still thinking about the mansaf. The thing that keeps coming back to me is that she learned all of it here, in this kitchen, from watching me. Which means even if she's somewhere else, she carries it. I don't know why that's so hard for me to trust.

My father never let any of us leave Amman until we were married. I accepted that. But I also remember the years I spent wondering what it would have been like to study abroad when I had the chance. I told myself it didn't matter because I had responsibilities. Maybe it didn't matter. Or maybe I just never forgave myself for not pushing harder, and now I'm doing the same thing to her.

Nudge: "Is this about Noor, or about your own regret?"

Response: Maybe I'm afraid her thriving proves I didn't need to sacrifice everything.

Eid preparations started today and the house was full. Hana brought her husband and the baby, Tariq came with sweets from that bakery on Rainbow Street, even Walid video-called from Dubai to wish us well. Noor was in the kitchen all morning helping me roll grape leaves. For a few hours it felt exactly right, all the noise, the food, children running through every room. This is what I stayed home for.

But then after everyone left and it was just Noor and me cleaning up, she said quietly that she'd been offered a partial scholarship. She wasn't asking permission, not exactly, more like she was letting me know it was real now. I didn't say anything. I just kept drying the dishes and stacking them slowly.

I know I need to talk to her. I know what I'm leaning toward. But saying it out loud makes it final in a way I'm not ready for. The house will be so quiet without her.

Told Noor this morning she should accept the scholarship. She cried. I cried. We sat at the kitchen table for a long time not saying much. She kept saying she'd come back every summer, and I told her she should come back when she wants to, not because she feels she has to.

Spent the rest of the day reorganizing the pantry because I needed to do something with my hands. Found a jar of Samira's pickled turnips behind everything, must be from Hana's last batch. Put it on the counter where I can see it. The house is the same but it already feels like it's shifting.

Nudge: "Why put that jar where you can see it?"

Noor's been researching London neighborhoods all week, asking me to help her compare rental listings. Funny how she still wants my opinion on every detail even though she's the one leaving. I caught myself actually enjoying it, looking up transport maps, checking which areas have halal shops nearby. Like I'm planning a trip I'll never take.

Tariq suggested we start a family group chat so Noor can send photos when she's there. I said fine but I'm not learning any new apps. He laughed. I think this is how it goes, you hold something tightly and then you open your hand and it doesn't disappear. It just changes shape. I'm not all the way there yet but I can see the outline of it.

Nudge: "What does that outline actually look like?"

Marking forty-two exam papers and my eyes are giving out. Priya from Class 10 wrote something genuinely moving about her grandmother's village, but I cannot even enjoy it properly because the stack keeps growing and dinner still needs sorting.

Mummy-ji asked twice when I would be done with the table because she wants to lay out her medicines. I said ten more minutes both times and it was never ten minutes. Rajesh heated leftovers for everyone without being asked, which was something. Still at the table now. Still marking.

Nudge: "Who got the short end tonight?"

Response: Everyone, probably. The papers got half my attention, Mummy-ji got empty promises, and I did not even sit with Rajesh while he ate.

Parent-teacher meeting ran an hour over. Sharmaji spent twenty minutes arguing his son deserves higher marks in poetry analysis. I explained the rubric twice. He was not satisfied but eventually left.

Came home to find the washing machine had leaked again. Rajesh called the repair person. Bahu made chai without asking, which was nice. Ate late. Nothing else to say about it.

Same day but lying in bed now and actually feeling lighter than expected. The chai Bahu made — she added elaichi the way I like it without me mentioning. Small thing but it sat with me.

Nudge: "What made that land so much?"

Response: She noticed. Nobody asked her to, she just paid attention. Sometimes that is the whole thing.

Ritu called during lunch break, which never happens on weekdays, and for a second my chest seized because I thought something was wrong with the baby. But no — she just wanted to tell me that little Aarav said "Nani" for the first time. Clear as anything, she said, not a babble. She held the phone to him and he did it again and I was standing in the staff room corridor pressing the phone to my ear like an idiot with tears running.

The other teachers walked past and Sunita asked if everything was okay. I just nodded and waved her off. Could not explain it properly. Twenty-eight years of students calling me Meena ma'am and none of it hits the way one small boy saying Nani does.

Came home and told Mummy-ji and she got teary too. We sat together for a bit, just the two of us, which does not happen often these days because the house is always full of someone needing something. Rajesh came in and saw us and quietly backed out. He knows when to leave a room. That is its own kind of gift, knowing when not to interrupt.

Spent the morning helping Sunita draft her transfer application — she wants to move closer to her aging parents in Varanasi. Felt right, sitting with her and getting the wording correct, even though it means the department loses a good teacher.

Nudge: "You're helping her leave. Does that sting at all?"

Response: It is her parents, what else would I tell her. Anyway the application needed better phrasing, that was the immediate problem.

Staff meeting about the new attendance software. Everyone complained. I kept quiet even though I also find it needlessly complicated — did not see the point in adding to the noise when the decision was already made.

Sunday reading circle went well. Kamla-ji finally came back after skipping three weeks because of her knee. She said she almost did not come today either but her daughter-in-law pushed her out the door. We are reading a Premchand collection and she had opinions about Nirmala that got everyone arguing in the best way.

I had set up an extra cushion near the door in case her knee was bad, and she noticed and patted my hand when she sat down. We have known each other since our children were in primary school. There is something about that length of knowing someone — you can set up a cushion without it being a fuss.

After everyone left I cleaned up the chai cups and rearranged the chairs. Rajesh offered to help but I told him to rest, his back has been bothering him. Did it myself in fifteen minutes. The room still smelled like cardamom and old pages. Good Sunday.

Neha from the English department asked if I would co-sign a letter to the principal about the new workload policy — extra periods without compensation. She has been drafting it for weeks and already has four signatures. I said I would think about it.

I agree with every word in that letter. The workload is unreasonable and the younger teachers are burning out. But the principal and I go back years, he came to Ritu's wedding, and this feels like it would land as a personal thing even if it is not meant that way. Neha looked disappointed when I did not sign immediately. She did not say anything but the pause said enough.

Now I am sitting here turning it over and I do not like how I handled that. The right thing and the comfortable thing are not the same here and I walked toward comfortable without even deciding to. I keep telling my students to speak up when something matters. Easier to say from behind a desk apparently.

Still have not signed Neha's letter. Rajesh says I am overthinking it and should just do what feels right, but that is exactly the problem — what feels right is staying out of it, and I am not sure that is actually right. Not everything needs to be a stand.

Nudge: "What would signing cost you, specifically?"

Response: It is not about cost. It is about... I do not know. Can we not do this right now.

A former coworker called me today. She left the hospital two years ago to do travel nursing out in California and she's making almost double what I make. She told me about surfing on her days off, about renting this little bungalow in San Diego with ocean sounds at night. She wasn't bragging, just talking. But something about the way she described her mornings hit me in a way I wasn't ready for.

I almost asked her how the contracts work. I got as far as opening a browser tab. Then Ellie came in asking for help with her spelling homework and I closed it. I told myself it was because the timing was wrong, but really I think I was afraid of what I'd find out. That it might actually be doable. That's the part I don't want to sit with.

Nudge: "What were you afraid you'd find out?"

Response: That the money works. That Kyle could find jobs out there. That the kids are young enough to adjust. Basically that there's no real reason not to except that I don't want to give up what I already know.

Meal prepped for the whole week. Got the kids' lunches sorted through Friday. Kyle fixed the dryer vent that's been rattling since October. Checked the HSA balance and we're on track to max it out by March. Nothing special about today but everything's where it should be.

Kyle's buddy offered us his cabin up north for New Year's, last minute, like they always do. Free stay, just bring food. Kyle's face lit up and the kids were already asking about the lake. And I wanted to say yes. I really did. But the drive is four hours each way in December, the roads up past Duluth are unpredictable, we'd have to pull the kids out of their routine, pack for cold weather, hope the cabin's pipes aren't frozen.

Kyle said I was overthinking it. Maybe I am. But somebody has to think about whether the car insurance covers off-road conditions up there, whether we'd have cell service if something happened, whether the kids' asthma inhalers are enough if we're that far from the nearest hospital.

I said no. Or I said "I don't think it's a good idea right now" which is the same thing but lets me pretend I'm being reasonable instead of just scared. Kyle shrugged and said okay, but his shoulders dropped. The kids went back to their tablets. And now I'm sitting here wondering if I just took something good away from them because the version of the weekend that sounded fun was also the version I couldn't fully control.

I keep doing this. Choosing the safe answer and then feeling lousy about it after.

Nudge: "When's the last time you said yes to something like that?"

Worked a double because two people called out. New Year's Eve in the ICU is always rough. Had a guy come in from an ATV accident, 24 years old, and it was touch and go for a few hours. He stabilized around 10 PM. His girlfriend was in the waiting room the whole time, shaking.

Got home at one in the morning and Kyle had left a plate in the microwave. The kids were asleep. There was confetti on the kitchen floor from whatever small celebration they had without me. I ate standing up at the counter, too tired to sit down properly. Didn't feel like New Year's. Felt like any other late shift except the drive home had more drunk drivers to watch out for.

Going to bed now. Tomorrow is another shift.

Woke up and saw a group text from nursing school friends. Half of them have moved on to different things. One's a nurse practitioner in Portland. One left healthcare entirely and does medical device sales now, making three times what I make. And here I am about to drive back to the same unit for another twelve-hour shift. Something about New Year's makes this sting more than usual.

Nudge: "Is it the money or the change that stings?"

Response: Probably both. I don't know. Forget it, I'm just tired from the double yesterday.

First full normal week back after the holidays. Got into a good rhythm. Three day shifts in a row, all manageable patient loads. Came home each night by 7:30, had dinner with the family, helped Owen with his reading log.

Kyle and I sat down Sunday morning and went through the January budget. Mortgage is on autopay, car payment goes through Friday, we've got enough set aside for the furnace inspection next month. Put fifty extra into the emergency fund. Not a lot, but consistent.

The house felt right this week. Quiet mornings, packed lunches on the counter, everyone knowing where they need to be. I like when things click like that. I know it won't always be this smooth, but when it is, I want to just let myself notice it.

Slow day at work, which is rare. Spent most of my downtime restocking supply carts and updating patient charts. One of the newer nurses asked me if I ever get bored doing the same unit for this long. I told her no without thinking about it. Not sure that was completely honest.

Still thinking about what that nurse asked me today. Bored. Like it's about entertainment. I'm not here to be entertained. I'm here because the pay is solid, the benefits are excellent, and I know the protocols well enough that I can keep people alive in their worst moments without second-guessing myself. That counts for something.

But then I drove home and passed that new urgent care clinic they're building off Highway 7 and I thought about how a friend from my cohort just became the clinical director at one of those places. Regular hours. No nights. She said it's like a different career. And I felt something I didn't want to feel, which was jealousy. Not at the title or the money, but at the fact that she let herself want something different and then actually went after it.

I don't know why that's so hard for me. Kyle would support it. My manager would write me a recommendation. The kids would barely notice. There's nothing actually stopping me except that the idea of starting over somewhere new, learning new workflows, being the person who doesn't know where anything is, makes my stomach drop. I'd rather stay where I can do the job with my eyes closed than go somewhere I'd have to prove myself all over again.

That's not ambition. But I'm not sure it's laziness either. It's just where I am.

Nudge: "What's the worst that happens if you're the new person somewhere?"

Response: I think the worst thing isn't failing. It's that I'd miss the version of myself that had everything figured out. Even if that version is just standing still.

Long week but a good one. Had a patient who'd been in the unit for twelve days finally get transferred to a regular room. His wife hugged me in the hallway and I had to duck into the break room for a minute after because it hit harder than I expected.

Kyle picked up the kids from school all week so I could take the early shifts. The fridge is stocked, the laundry's done, the mortgage auto-drafted on time. Owen lost a tooth at school on Wednesday and the tooth fairy remembered on the first night for once.

I'm tired in my body but not in my head tonight. That's the best kind of tired. Tomorrow's Saturday and I'm off. No plans except pancakes and whatever the kids want to do. That's enough.
